title: GenAI-Powered Text Personalization: Natural Language Processing Validation of Adaptation Capabilities
venue: Applied Sciences (Switzerland)
justification: The article meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2025 (CI1), in English (CI2), it is a journal article in Applied Sciences (CI3), uses LLMs (Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Llama 3.1, Gemini Pro 1.5, ChatGPT-4) to generate and adapt scientific didactic text content (biology, chemistry, physics) personalised for different reader profiles (CI4), employs structured prompting and RAG as adaptation techniques (CI5), and the full text is available (CI6). The study directly addresses the personalisation of educational materials through LLMs with NLP validation, in line with the TFM on controlled generation of didactic material.
llm_base: Claude 3.5 Sonnet; Llama 3.1; Gemini Pro 1.5; ChatGPT-4 (GPT-4o)
adaptation_technique: Prompting (single-shot and augmented with personification, chain-of-thought, RAG) for personalisation of scientific texts according to reader profiles
dataset: 10 scientific texts on biology, chemistry, and physics adapted to 4 hypothetical reader profiles (varying prior knowledge, reading ability, and learning objectives)
output_format: Adapted educational text (paragraphs of scientific texts modified for different levels of complexity and cohesion)
evaluation_method: Automatic NLP with Writing Analytics Tool (WAT): readability metrics (FKGL), conceptual density, cohesion, syntactic and lexical complexity; human evaluation of quality with a rubric (reader-text alignment, comprehension, engagement)
educational_level: Not explicitly specified; hypothetical profiles range from beginner to advanced readers with university-level knowledge
key_findings: LLMs vary significantly in their ability to adjust linguistic features according to reader profiles (Exp. 1). Prompting augmented with RAG, chain-of-thought, and personification substantially improves profile-text alignment (Exp. 2). NLP proves to be a scalable and efficient method for real-time validation of content personalisation generated by GenAI, allowing iterative refinement without costly human studies. Claude and ChatGPT show better performance in cohesion adjustment for low- and high-knowledge readers respectively.

title: Steerable Generative AI-Based Virtual Tutor for Design Learning
venue: ICCE-Taiwan - IEEE Int. Conf. Consum. Electron. - Taiwan: Gener. AI Innov. Consum. Technol., Proc.
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: (CI1) published in 2025; (CI2) in English; (CI3) IEEE conference paper (ICCE-Taiwan); (CI4) LLM applied to industrial design tutoring for didactic purposes; (CI5) explicit fine-tuning with LoRA on Llama-3-Taiwan-8B; (CI6) full text available. No exclusion criterion applies: it is not an editorial, not a review/survey, not in the medical domain, and uses the LLM as the core of the system. The system virtualises a master designer (Philippe Starck) through a fine-tuned LLM to provide personalised dialogic learning to Taiwanese industrial design students via a mixed-reality device (Meta Quest 3).
adaptation_technique: Fine-tuning with LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation); dataset prepared from interview transcripts, official web content, and portfolios of the master designer
fine_tuning_method: LoRA (rank 8, alpha 16, batch size 16, 30 epochs, lr 0.003, dropout 0.05)
dataset: Proprietary dataset built from Philippe Starck's materials: interview transcripts, official web content (bibliographies, portfolios); tokenised as a prompt dataset for NLP
output_format: Text responses generated by the fine-tuned LLM, converted to speech in Mandarin via VITS/VITS2 and presented through a dynamic avatar in a mixed-reality environment (Unity / Meta Quest 3)
evaluation_method: Manual evaluation by industrial design professionals: >90% agreement with the master's original materials on a 30% test split of the dataset
educational_level: Higher education (university level); industrial design students in Taiwan
key_findings: A LoRA fine-tuned LLM trained on original expert materials can effectively emulate the voice and design knowledge of a recognised master designer (>90% acceptability). Integrating speech recognition (Whisper), expressive speech synthesis (VITS2), and mixed reality (Meta Quest 3) produces an authentic and personalised learning experience. The steerable AI approach allows control over pedagogical style and teaching persona through the selection and preparation of the fine-tuning corpus. The system demonstrates viability as a proof of concept for expert knowledge transfer in creative design education.

title: Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning of LLMs Enhanced with RAG to Assist Students with Lectures
venue: Proc. ICITR - Int. Conf. Inf. Technol. Res.: Adv. Glob. Connect. Through Emerg. Inf. Technol. Solut.
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: (CI1) published in 2025 at ICITR; (CI2) in English; (CI3) IEEE conference paper; (CI4) uses LLMs to generate didactic material (structured lecture notes, Q&A, question generation) assisting students with readings; (CI5) explicitly and in detail combines PEFT/LoRA (fine-tuning) with RAG; (CI6) full text available. No exclusion criterion applies: it is not an editorial, not a review/survey, not in the medical domain, and the LLM is central to the system.
adaptation_technique: PEFT with LoRA (Supervised Fine-Tuning of Low-Rank Adapters, rank=8, LoRA alpha=32) + RAG (ChromaDB as vector store, all-MiniLM-L6-V2 as embedding model, LangChain, dual-mode retrieval: metadata filtering + top-k similarity)
fine_tuning_method: LoRA (PEFT/SFT) with the PEFT and TRL libraries on a free Google Colab GPU
dataset: Author-created dataset: 500 short transcript examples (~15 min) and 124 long transcript examples (~30 min), with systematic paraphrasing as augmentation. Manually created gold-standard notes. Set of 100 questions for RAG evaluation.
output_format: Structured lecture notes in bullet points, answers to questions about lecture content, questions generated from past readings
evaluation_method: ROUGE-1/2/L (lexical accuracy), BERTScore-F1 (semantic similarity), Perplexity (before/after fine-tuning), RAGAS metrics (context precision 0.9857, context recall 0.9571, faithfulness 0.7679, answer relevancy 0.9351, semantic similarity 0.9224, answer correctness 0.8377), hallucination rate (6.33% with RAG vs. 43.33% without RAG)
educational_level: Higher university education (University of Vavuniya, Sri Lanka)
key_findings: The phi3-lecture_note-ft model (Phi-3-mini-4k-Instruct fine-tuned with LoRA) outperformed Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct in lecture note generation. RAG integration reduced the hallucination rate by 37% (from 43.33% to 6.33%). RAGAS metrics show high retriever precision and recall (>0.98). The study demonstrates that small models (<4B params) efficiently fine-tuned with PEFT+RAG are viable on low-cost hardware. Increasing the LLM context window without RAG does not improve handling of long texts; RAG is the key strategy. The full pipeline (ASR + PEFT-LLM + RAG) operates end-to-end on modest hardware.

title: AI-GENERATED LEARNING RESOURCES FOR CREATIVE FIELDS
venue: ShodhKosh: Journal of Visual and Performing Arts
justification: Paper proposes a modular AI architecture for generating personalized, multimodal didactic resources in creative fields (art, design, music, media, architecture), explicitly incorporating fine-tuning pipelines and personalization mechanisms based on learner behavior. Satisfies CI1 (LLMs/generative AI central), CI2 (educational context), CI3 (fine-tuning + personalization method), CI4 (generation of didactic material), CI5 (2026), and CI6 (open access).
llm_base: large language models; diffusion models; neural audio synthesis
adaptation_technique: fine-tuning; personalization; adaptive recommendations; learner profiling
fine_tuning_method: Fine-tuning pipelines on creative domain datasets with personalization based on learner behavior and performance data
dataset: Creative datasets, artistic workflows, metadata and annotations (domain-specific creative knowledge repositories)
output_format: tutorials; design prompts; visual content; audio/music; feedback and critiques
evaluation_method: quantitative measures of learning effectiveness; qualitative user studies with students and educators
educational_level: Higher education / lifelong learning (creative fields: art, design, music, media, architecture)
rq_coverage: RQ1-fine-tuning for didactic generation; RQ2-personalization; RQ3-multimodal output; RQ4-evaluation of educational quality
key_findings: AI-generated learning resources using fine-tuned generative models improve engagement, conceptual knowledge, and creative efficiency in creative education; modular architecture combining knowledge repositories, fine-tuning pipelines, and learner profiling enables personalized multimodal content generation; critical issues include pedagogical alignment, quality assurance, cultural bias, and over-reliance on auto-generated content.

title: LLaMa-Based Personalized Learning: Fine-Tuning Generative AI in Educational Recommendations
venue: SECOND INTERNATIONAL SYMPOSIUM ON GENERATIVE AI AND EDUCATION, ISGAIE 2025
justification: The paper fine-tunes LLaMA (with RLHF) on K-12 and OpenEdX datasets to generate personalized educational recommendations, directly addressing CI4 and CI5. It is a 2025 conference paper (ISGAIE 2025, Springer LNDECT) in English with an original empirical study, meeting CI1-CI6. The educational context is K-12 / e-learning and the work explicitly evaluates personalization per student profile.
adaptation_technique: fine-tuning
fine_tuning_method: RLHF
dataset: K-12 educational datasets and OpenEdX learning platform (50,000 students, 1,200 courses, 2.5M interactions)
output_format: personalized learning resource recommendations (ranked list)
evaluation_method: automatic
key_findings: Fine-tuned LLaMA achieves NDCG@10 of 0.754, outperforming hybrid CF+CBF (0.689) by leveraging transformer-based contextual embeddings; the model excels in cold-start and sparse data scenarios. Future work should address computational cost, fairness, and real-time adaptability.

title: A Novel Approach to Scalable and Automatic Topic-Controlled Question Generation in Education
venue: Int. Conf. Learn. Anal. Knowl., LAK
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: conference paper (LAK 2025) published in 2025, in English, presenting a topic-controlled automatic question generation system (T-CQG) through fine-tuning of a small language model (T5-small) with datasets specifically created for education. It applies supervised fine-tuning, quantisation (QLoRA, LLM.int8), and data augmentation. It generates didactic content (questions) and evaluates educational quality with automatic metrics and human evaluation. It is not a review, editorial, or in the medical domain.
adaptation_technique: Supervised fine-tuning with a contrastive learning dataset (MixSQuAD); additional pretraining on scientific texts (PeS2O); 8-bit quantisation (LLM.int8) and 4-bit quantisation (QLoRA); data augmentation (MixSQuAD2X)
fine_tuning_method: Supervised fine-tuning with contrastive examples on T5-small; variants with post-training quantisation using LLM.int8 (TopicQG8bit) and QLoRA (TopicQG4bit)
dataset: SQuAD 1.1 (base), extended to SQuAD+ (wikification + topic annotation), then to MixSQuAD (contrastive pairs, 10,000 examples), and finally to MixSQuAD2X (augmented, 20,000 examples); evaluation on MixKhanQ (derived from KhanQ, 1,034 STEM questions from Khan Academy)
output_format: Open-ended, topic-controlled questions in text format; answer-agnostic
evaluation_method: Automatic linguistic quality metrics (BLEU, METEOR, ROUGE, F1, Perplexity) + topical relevance metrics (BERTScore without stopwords, WikiSemRel based on Wikipedia/WAT API) + human annotator evaluation (MAE between annotators and SemRel score, n=4 annotators, 30 questions)
educational_level: General (primary, secondary, MOOC, university); examples drawn from Khan Academy (K-12/STEM) and SQuAD (Wikipedia); emphasis on personalised tutoring systems and LMS
rq_coverage: RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_findings: Fine-tuning T5-small with contrastive examples (MixSQuAD) produces high-quality, topically relevant educational questions that surpass the baseline. Quantisation to 4-bit reduces the model to 94 MB with minimal performance loss, making the system viable on mobile devices without relying on proprietary LLMs such as ChatGPT. Additional pretraining on scientific texts (TopicQGedu) improves educational terminology. WikiSemRel is proposed as a topical semantic evaluation metric, validated by high inter-annotator agreement. The system is scalable and can be integrated into LMS and ITS platforms for personalised question generation.

title: LPITutor: An LLM based personalized intelligent tutoring system using RAG and prompt engineering
venue: PeerJ Computer Science
justification: LPITutor uses RAG and prompt engineering with a pre-trained GPT-3.5 model accessed via API with no fine-tuning applied; the system generates personalized tutoring dialogue responses (chatbot-style ITS) rather than structured didactic material in multiple formats, falling outside CI4 (generation of teaching material).
adaptation_technique: RAG; prompt engineering
fine_tuning_method: none
dataset: Course documents embedded in a vector database (sentence-transformer encoders)
output_format: conversational tutoring responses
evaluation_method: accuracy; completeness; clarity; difficulty alignment; coherence; relevance
educational_level: not specified (general ITS for students)
key_findings: LPITutor integrates RAG + adaptive prompt engineering over GPT-3.5 (API, no fine-tuning) to deliver personalized tutoring responses adapted to learner skill level and query complexity; no fine-tuning or generation of structured didactic material formats is involved.

title: ReviseSmart: An AI-Powered Personalized Revision Platform
venue: IEEE Int. Conf. Intelligent Signal Processing Effective Communication Technologies, INSPECT
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2025 (CI1), in English (CI2), IEEE conference paper (CI3), applies LLMs to generate personalised didactic material, including courses, quizzes, and summaries (CI4), employs fine-tuning (LoRA/QLoRA on LLaMA-2) and structured prompting as adaptation techniques (CI5), and the full text is available (CI6). No exclusion criterion applies: it is not an editorial, not a review/survey, it does not belong to the medical domain, and the central focus is LLMs for education.
adaptation_technique: Fine-tuning (LoRA + QLoRA) and structured prompting (prompt-engineered queries)
fine_tuning_method: LoRA + QLoRA on NousResearch/Llama-2-7b-chat-hf; r=64, alpha=16, dropout=0.1; 1 epoch, batch 4, lr 2e-4, 250 steps
dataset: mlabonne/guanaco-llama2-1k (general instruction dataset for fine-tuning)
output_format: MCQs (multiple-choice questions), open-ended questions, video transcript summaries, structured course outlines
evaluation_method: Controlled study (n=40, experimental vs. control): pre-test/post-test with two-sample t-test; satisfaction survey; system performance metrics (generation time, standard deviation)
educational_level: Higher education (undergraduate) and self-directed learning; implicitly applicable to secondary level as well
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ7
key_findings: ReviseSmart is a modular personalised revision platform that integrates fine-tuning (LoRA/QLoRA) of LLaMA-2 for generating quizzes and courses from documents and YouTube videos. The experimental group showed a 32% learning improvement versus 12% in the control group (p<0.05) and 87% satisfaction. The architecture combines Cohere and Gemini for parsing with fine-tuned LLaMA-2 for question generation, achieving personalisation according to the student's profile and materials.

title: Design and Optimization of an Offline AI Model for Enhancing Student Learning and Academic Assistance
venue: IEEE Int. Conf. Sustain. Technol. Ind. 5.0, STI
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: year 2025 (CI1), English (CI2), IEEE conference paper (CI3), LLM applied to offline academic educational assistance (CI4), fine-tuning with LoRA plus quantisation on OpenStax/Alpaca educational data (CI5), full text available (CI6). No exclusion criterion applies: not an editorial, not a review, not in the medical domain, and uses LLM with specific adaptation techniques.
adaptation_technique: Fine-tuning with LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) plus 4-bit GGUF quantisation via llama.cpp for offline deployment on consumer hardware
fine_tuning_method: LoRA (instruction tuning with instruction-response pairs derived from OpenStax and Alpaca; r=8, alpha=16, dropout=0.05, AdamW, 3 epochs per chunk); post-merge Q4_0 GGUF quantisation
dataset: OpenStax textbooks (Physics, Biology, Chemistry) + Stanford Alpaca dataset; custom dataset structured as instruction-output pairs; 70/15/15 split; ~16 GB RAM MacBook Pro
output_format: Textual responses in natural language aligned with academic curriculum (step-by-step explanations, textbook-style Q&A); desktop interface via tkinter and a local llama.cpp server
evaluation_method: BLEU (sacreBLEU), ROUGE-L (rouge_score) on 1000 test samples; inference latency (seconds); RAM usage (GB); small human evaluation (10 educators, 100 responses, 1-5 scale for factuality and clarity); comparison with Phi-3 Mini, Mistral 7B, Gemma 7B, OpenHermes 2.5 (all 4-bit offline); statistical t-tests (p<0.05)
educational_level: Secondary and introductory university education (OpenStax content: Physics, Biology, Chemistry)
key_findings: LLaMA 3.1-8B fine-tuned with LoRA on OpenStax+Alpaca educational data and quantised to Q4_0 GGUF (4.2 GB) outperforms Phi-3 Mini, Mistral 7B, Gemma 7B, and OpenHermes 2.5 in BLEU (86.9 vs 72.1-77.8) and ROUGE-L (96.5 vs 88.4-92.5) on educational QA, with a latency of 2.0 s and a memory footprint of 4.2 GB on a MacBook Pro with 16 GB RAM. Quantisation reduces the model size from 16.1 GB to 4.2 GB with minimal degradation (under 2 BLEU points). The system is fully offline and reproducible, demonstrating viability for educational environments without internet connectivity.

title: Agentic RAG for Personalized Learning: Design of an AI-Powered Learning Agent Using Open-Source Small Language Models
venue: Electronic Journal of e-Learning
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: year 2025 (CI1), English (CI2), peer-reviewed journal article in Electronic Journal of e-Learning (CI3), applies LLM/SLM (gemma 2:2b, Llava, minicpm-v) for personalised didactic material generation from OERs, including summaries, flashcards, and quizzes (CI4), the core technique is Agentic RAG with structured prompting via LlamaIndex (CI5), and the full text is available (CI6). Not a review, not in the medical domain, not an editorial.
adaptation_technique: Agentic RAG (LlamaIndex Router Query Engine with List Index, Vector Store Index, and Multimodal Vector Store Index); structured prompting per tool (Summary Tool, Flashcard Tool, Quiz Tool, Retrieval Tool, Image QA Tool)
dataset: Open Educational Resources (OERs) provided by the learner at runtime; evaluation on 4 public OERs (economics, equity, respiratory anatomy, etc.)
output_format: Text summaries, flashcards, multiple-choice quizzes, answers to contextual questions, answers to visual questions (image QA)
evaluation_method: BERT Score (semantic similarity of summaries generated by the agent versus ChatGPT GPT-4o); qualitative analysis of correct tool selection by the Router (25 questions on 4 OERs, score 0-2 per question)
educational_level: Not specified (self-directed learning with OERs; implicit university context; also mentioned for use on low-cost devices accessible to learners in general)
key_findings: The agentic RAG agent with locally running open-source SLMs via Ollama selected the correct tool autonomously in virtually all queries. Summaries achieved high semantic similarity with GPT-4o outputs according to BERT Score, showing that lightweight SLMs can deliver near-state-of-the-art performance with full learner privacy, no cloud infrastructure cost, and multimodal support (text plus images). The design is reproducible and the implementation guides are publicly available.

title: LightRAG-Enhanced Automated Question Generation System for Multi-Domain Educational Assessments
venue: Int. Workshop Artif. Intell. Educ., WAIE
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2025 (CI1), English (CI2), IEEE WAIE 2025 conference paper (CI3), uses LLM (GPT-4 via OpenAI API) for educational material generation, specifically MCQs (CI4), employs advanced RAG (LightRAG with graph-based text indexing and dual-level retrieval) as an LLM adaptation technique (CI5), and full text is available (CI6). Not a review or survey, not an editorial, not in the medical domain. The system generates multi-domain educational assessment questions aligned with Bloom's Taxonomy.
adaptation_technique: RAG (LightRAG: graph-based text indexing and dual-level retrieval with local and global keywords; Hybrid LightRAG vs. Naïve LightRAG)
dataset: 240 MCQs generated across two custom domains: Electronics Engineering (120) and Engineering Sciences (120), distributed across 4 topics each with 5 items per Bloom level; educational PDF documents (textbooks, lecture notes) uploaded by the user as a dynamic source
output_format: Multiple-choice questions (MCQs) with correct answer, distractors, and explanation; exported in DOCX format compatible with Canvas LMS
evaluation_method: Binary rubric (0/1) on 5 criteria: accuracy, relevance, grammatical correctness, cognitive level alignment (Bloom's Taxonomy), diversity; evaluators: 3 human experts and 3 LLMs (ChatGPT, Gemini, DeepSeek)
educational_level: Higher education (Electronics Engineering, Engineering Sciences; level not explicitly stated but university context at SP Jain School of Global Management)
key_findings: The Hybrid LightRAG model outperforms Naive LightRAG in accuracy (95% vs. 93% LLM; 94% vs. 91% human in EE) and diversity (100% vs. 94%), with 100% Bloom alignment in both models. LightRAG addresses the limitations of traditional RAG (contextual fragmentation, poor multi-domain adaptability) through knowledge graph indexing and dual retrieval (local+global keywords). The greatest advantage appears at the Application level (86% vs. 72%). Main limitation: numerical reasoning and multi-step computation. Future work targets symbolic reasoning, flashcard generation, automatic summaries, and pilot deployment in real environments.

title: CogniLearn: Integrating AI-Powered Insights for Class 10 Syllabus
venue: Proc. - Int. Conf. Comput. Artif. Intell., ICCAI
justification: CogniLearn fine-tunes LLaMA-2 7B and Qwen 0.5B using LoRA and QLoRA combined with RAG and prompt engineering to generate exam-aligned question-answer pairs for Class 10 CBSE students, directly addressing CI1-CI5. The study evaluates output quality with BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore, and cosine similarity, and contextualizes responses to specific syllabus topics, contributing to RQ2-RQ7.
adaptation_technique: fine-tuning; LoRA; QLoRA; RAG; prompt engineering; few-shot learning; chain-of-thought prompting
fine_tuning_method: SFT with LoRA and QLoRA (4-bit quantization); LoRA rank 64, alpha 16, dropout 0.1; 4 epochs, batch size 4, lr 2e-4
dataset: Custom proprietary dataset of ~3,000 QA pairs from CBSE Class 10 Science, Social Studies, and English textbooks and past exam question papers
output_format: question-answering (short answer, MCQ, long answer)
evaluation_method: BLEU; ROUGE; BERTScore; cosine similarity; Word Mover's Distance (WMD)
educational_level: Secondary school (Class 10, CBSE India)
rq_coverage: RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_findings: Fine-tuning with LoRA/QLoRA improves smaller models (Qwen 0.5B) more than larger ones (LLaMA-2 7B); RAG consistently enhances both models; Qwen fine-tuned + RAG matches LLaMA-2 performance despite 14x fewer parameters; role-based and chain-of-thought prompt engineering further aligns responses to board exam standards.

title: Pedagogy-R1: Pedagogical Large Reasoning Model and Well-balanced Educational Benchmark
venue: CIKM - Proc. ACM Int. Conf. Info. Knowl. Manage.
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2025 (CIKM), English, conference paper, proposes an LLM framework for authentic pedagogical tasks in education, uses fine-tuning by distillation (instruction tuning on DeepSeek-R1/QwQ-32B) with pedagogical filtering, and introduces a prompting strategy (Chain-of-Pedagogy). Not a review or survey, not in the medical domain, and the full text is available.
adaptation_technique: Distillation-based instruction tuning with pedagogical filtering (Pedagogical Reasoning) and Chain-of-Pedagogy (CoP) prompting
fine_tuning_method: Instruction tuning by distillation on filtered outputs of QwQ-32B as teacher model; pipeline: data generation, filtering by correctness (D_filtered), instruction tuning of small models (1.5B and 7B)
dataset: Well-balanced Educational Benchmark (WBEB): custom dataset built by the authors with 5 categories (Subject Knowledge, Pedagogical Knowledge, Knowledge Tracing, Automated Essay Scoring, Real-World Teacher Decision-Making); curated public and private data, some translated with GPT-4.1-mini
output_format: Pedagogical responses in natural language: formative feedback, step-by-step explanations, teacher decision-making, essay scoring (MCQA, Binary Classification, Open-ended scoring)
evaluation_method: WBEB benchmark (5 dimensions); quantitative metrics: accuracy (ACC), AUC; qualitative analysis via Grounded Theory coding; comparison with base models, reasoning models (DeepSeek-R1 family, GPT-4.1, o4-mini), and models with standard instruction tuning
educational_level: K-12 and university (multiple levels depending on subdataset: elementary, middle, secondary, university)
key_findings: Pedagogy-R1 (1.5B and 7B) outperforms base models and general reasoning models on pedagogical tasks (PK, AES, DM) when combined with CoP prompting, demonstrating that domain-specific instruction tuning significantly improves educational reasoning. The 7B model with CoP achieves +31.34 points in DM over the base model. The CoP strategy, both in training data and at inference time, is key to eliciting coherent pedagogical judgements. The models remain below specialist performance on Knowledge Tracing (AUC).

title: YMCQ: Reasoning-Enhanced MCQ Generation
venue: ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE IN EDUCATION, AIED 2025, PT VI
justification: Paper presents YMCQ, a method for automated MCQ generation that fine-tunes an open-source LLM (Llama 3.1 8B-Instruct) on publicly available educational datasets augmented with synthetically generated reasoning explanations. Published 2025 (AIED 2025), English, conference paper. Directly applies LLM fine-tuning for didactic content generation (MCQs), meets CI1-CI6. Not a survey, not medical, not out-of-scope.
adaptation_technique: supervised fine-tuning with synthetic data augmentation (reasoning explanations); multi-step inference filtering pipeline
fine_tuning_method: full fine-tuning with mixed precision (bf16), AdamW-8bit optimizer, 1 epoch
dataset: SQuAD, HotpotQA, NarrativeQA, FairytaleQA, MCTest, RACE, EduGQ, augmented with synthetically generated reasoning explanations via Llama 3.1 70B (337,175 training / 27,164 validation examples)
output_format: MCQ (multiple-choice questions with distractors, reasoning explanations, and misconception-based distractors)
evaluation_method: pairwise human evaluation by 5 annotators; Bradley-Terry model for ranking; comparison against GPT-4o (zero-shot), model without explanations, and human-authored questions; iSTART ITS K-12 dataset used for evaluation
educational_level: K-12 (iSTART Intelligent Tutoring System dataset used for evaluation)
key_findings: Reasoning-enhanced fine-tuning of Llama 3.1 8B-Instruct improves MCQ and distractor quality over standard fine-tuning (without explanations). The method narrows the gap with human-authored questions and provides a cost-effective open-source alternative to GPT-4o. Multi-step filtering (entropy-based, similarity-based, factuality-checking) ensures validity and diversity of generated MCQs. Future work targets Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to further align generation with human judgment.

title: Enhancing Educational Content Through AI-Assisted Transformation of Video Transcriptions
venue: IEEE Digit. Educ. MOOCS Conf., DEMOcon
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2025 (IEEE DEMOcon), English, conference paper, uses LLMs (GPT-4o, GPT-4o mini, Gemini 2.0 Flash, Llama 3.3 70B) to generate multi-format didactic material (class notes, summaries, SEO abstracts, MCQ questions) from video transcriptions. The core technique is iterative and refined prompt engineering (CI5). Full text available. Not a review or survey, not medical, not an editorial.
llm_base: GPT-4o; GPT-4o mini; Gemini 2.0 Flash; Llama 3.3 70B
adaptation_technique: prompt engineering (iterative prompt refinement, sequential multi-step prompting, token-based length control)
dataset: 50,000+ educational videos from Universitat Politècnica de València (media.upv.es); transcriptions generated with WhisperX; 40 videos selected for comparative evaluation
output_format: detailed class notes, key-concept summaries, SEO-optimised abstracts, long-answer questions (paragraph-style), short-answer questions, MCQ questions with distractors
evaluation_method: human evaluation by three evaluators (1-10 utility scale); comparative analysis across models; automatic quality filtering by keywords; cost-per-token metrics
educational_level: university (higher education); institutional UPV repository with videos from university courses
key_findings: Gemini 2.0 Flash showed the best overall performance in pedagogical material generation (notes, summaries, abstracts) with utility scores between 7.9-9.0, and was also the most cost-effective model (~0.10€/M input tokens). Generating convincing MCQ distractors was the main challenge, requiring a separate two-step prompt that raised the average score from 4.5 to 5.75. The system processed 51,403 videos producing over 1 million questions. Human review before deployment remains indispensable to ensure pedagogical quality.

title: Automatic generation of introductory programming exercises with large language models
venue: Research and Practice in Technology Enhanced Learning
justification: Journal article (2026) in English. Proposes ExGen, an automatic programming exercise generation system for introductory courses using LLMs (GPT-3.5 Turbo and GPT-4 Turbo) with prompting strategies (zero-shot and few-shot) and self-filtering mechanisms. The generated didactic material consists of Python programming exercises with differentiated difficulty levels (easy/intermediate/hard) adapted to the student's level. Meets CI1 (2026), CI2 (EN), CI3 (journalArticle), CI4 (LLM for educational material generation), CI5 (structured prompting), CI6 (full text available). Not a review/survey, not an editorial, not in a medical domain.
adaptation_technique: Prompt engineering (zero-shot and few-shot prompting with keywords, difficulty levels, and example exercises as shots); self-filtering with code compilation, unit testing, and LLM-based difficulty classification
dataset: More than 1,400 Python exercises automatically generated and manually evaluated; seed exercise base from the university's introductory programming course
output_format: Python programming exercises (problem statement + reference solution + test cases) with three difficulty levels (easy, intermediate, hard)
evaluation_method: Empirical assessment of the ready-to-use exercise rate (>1,400 exercises); manual inspection by human experts; automatic comparison against human decisions; cost and generation-time analysis between GPT-3.5 Turbo and GPT-4 Turbo
educational_level: University (introductory programming course for first-year computer science students)
key_findings: ExGen generates ready-to-use Python exercises on demand for specific difficulty levels using few-shot prompting with keywords. The few-shot strategy is more precise than zero-shot. Self-filtering based on compilation, unit testing, and LLM classification reduces the need for manual inspection. GPT-4 Turbo outperforms GPT-3.5 Turbo on intermediate and hard exercises but is 20x more expensive and slower. Generating ready-to-use 'hard' exercises remains an open problem even with GPT-4 Turbo.

title: NOVA: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation Assistant in Spanish for Parallel Computing Education with Large Language Models
venue: Applied Sciences (Switzerland)
justification: Journal article (2025) in English presenting NOVA, an LLM+RAG-based educational virtual assistant for the Parallel Computing course at Universidad Politécnica Salesiana. The system uses GPT-4o mini with a Retrieval-Augmented Generation architecture and prompt engineering to generate Q&A, quizzes, and practical guides in Spanish. Fully meets CI1-CI6: original empirical paper, 2025, English, journal article, LLM applied to didactic content generation, RAG and prompting techniques, full text available. Not a review, not medical, not out-of-scope.
adaptation_technique: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) with vector database (Qdrant) for semantic retrieval + prompt engineering (prompt tuning, temperature control 0.2-0.35)
dataset: Curated domain-specific corpus: syllabus, practical exercises, class notes, and evaluations from the Parallel Computing course (4 units: CUDA, PyCUDA, message passing interface, clustering) at Universidad Politécnica Salesiana
output_format: Q&A (question-and-answer sessions), quizzes (MCQ-style), practical guides; conversational text responses in Spanish
evaluation_method: Test suite of 100 academic tasks (55 Q&A prompts, 25 practical guides, 20 quizzes); metrics: excellence rating (92%), retrieval coverage (72%), average response time (21 s); qualitative rubric scoring by development team
educational_level: University (undergraduate, Parallel Computing course)
rq_coverage: RQ2; RQ3; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7; RQ8
key_findings: NOVA achieved 92% excellence rating and 72% retrieval coverage across 100 test tasks, demonstrating that RAG architectures combined with pedagogically aligned corpora and prompt engineering can produce reliable, curriculum-grounded instructional content. The system operates entirely in Spanish, addressing an equity gap for Latin American STEM learners. No fine-tuning of the base LLM was performed; knowledge specialization is achieved solely through RAG and prompt tuning. Identified gap: lack of controlled user studies with real students and limited corpus coverage (72% retrieval). Future work includes multimodal support, adaptive feedback, and open-source LLM exploration.

title: Language Models for Educational Question Generation: Practical Challenges, Personalization Opportunities, and Parameter Optimization
venue: GENERATIVE SYSTEMS AND INTELLIGENT TUTORING SYSTEMS, ITS 2025, PT I
justification: Conference paper (2026, ITS 2025) in English that directly investigates LM-based educational question generation with fine-tuning of small language models (Gemma-7B, Gemma-7B-it) on course-specific data, systematic parameter optimization (granularity, scope, prompt style), and personalization via difficulty-level-matched question generation. Meets CI1-CI6.
adaptation_technique: Fine-tuning (supervised fine-tuning on domain-specific course materials) + prompt engineering (7 prompt styles including roleplaying, LM-generated prompts vs. human-created prompts)
fine_tuning_method: Supervised fine-tuning of small language models (SLMs <10B) on course-specific text data with varied granularity (page, paragraph, sentence) and scope (unit vs. course)
dataset: Custom dataset from an online software engineering course: SWEBOK textbook, scholarly papers, and web pages; manually cleaned and organized into page/paragraph/sentence granularities; no standard benchmark dataset used
output_format: Short-answer questions (open-ended) targeting specific difficulty levels (beginner, intermediate, advanced) aligned with Bloom's Taxonomy
evaluation_method: Difficulty matching rate (61.5%-67.4%), relevance, and quality scores; 7 prompt styles x 10 prompts + 9 human-generated prompts per setting; 84 experimental settings compared; NLP metrics (Perplexity, BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR) referenced from prior work
educational_level: Higher education / online university course (software engineering)
rq_coverage: RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_findings: Gemma-7B-it improved overall quality but struggled at beginner difficulty; sentence-level granularity with unit-only scope was optimal for training; roleplaying-style prompts produced best questions; LM-generated prompts outperformed human-created ones; prompt engineering was critical for LM control; considerable manual effort was needed to prepare domain-specific training data; difficulty-level personalization achieved only 61.5-67.4% accuracy, indicating room for improvement.

title: LOOKALIKE: Consistent Distractor Generation in Math MCQs
venue: PROCEEDINGS OF THE 20TH WORKSHOP ON INNOVATIVE USE OF NLP FOR BUILDING EDUCATIONAL APPLICATIONS, BEA 2025
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: conference paper (BEA 2025) from 2025 in English, applying SFT and DPO on MetaMath-Mistral 7B to generate consistent distractors for multiple-choice questions (MCQs) in mathematics for students aged 10-13. Uses fine-tuning techniques (SFT + DPO with alternating optimization) and generates educational didactic material (MCQ distractors with error explanations). Not a review/survey, not medical, full text available.
adaptation_technique: SFT + DPO (alternating optimization: supervised fine-tuning combined with Direct Preference Optimization using synthetic negative samples mined from model inconsistencies)
fine_tuning_method: SFT + DPO
dataset: Real-world math MCQ dataset from a large learning platform (hundreds of thousands of students); 1,434 MCQs for ages 10-13 covering 41 math subtopics (Arithmetic, Fractions, Solving Equations); each MCQ includes question stem, key, topic tags, 3 distractors, and teacher-written error descriptions; split 72%-16%-12% (train/val/test)
output_format: MCQ distractors (incorrect answer options) and error explanations for math multiple-choice questions
evaluation_method: Exact match (distractor generation accuracy); LLM-as-a-Judge with GPT-4o-mini (error generation, mathematical equivalence); human evaluation with ordinal ratings by two independent annotators with teaching experience; inter-annotator agreement via quadratic-weighted Cohen's kappa
educational_level: K-12 (middle school, ages 10-13)
rq_coverage: RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ8
key_findings: LOOKALIKE improves error-distractor consistency in math MCQs through two innovations: (1) mining synthetic preference pairs from the model's own inconsistencies, and (2) alternating SFT/DPO optimization to stabilize training. It achieves 51.6% accuracy in distractor generation and 57.2% in error generation (LLM-as-Judge), outperforming the state-of-the-art DiVERT (45.6%/47.7%). Human evaluation confirms that LOOKALIKE generates more consistent errors (0.587 vs 0.400 for SFT). Limitations: inconsistent examples persist and generalization to unseen topics remains unexplored.

title: Using Old Lessons for New AI - A Trainer for Project Risk Management
venue: FLEXIBLE QUERY ANSWERING SYSTEMS, FQAS 2025
justification: The paper presents an empirical case study of a triples-based fine-tuning methodology (instruction-tuning and alignment-tuning) applied to LLMs using existing Project Risk Management course materials as training data for Intelligent Tutoring Systems and Adaptive Learning Platforms. It addresses fine-tuning techniques, educational content generation, and personalization, satisfying all inclusion criteria. No exclusion criteria apply: it is an original empirical contribution (not a review), in an educational domain (not medical), and directly employs LLMs.
adaptation_technique: fine-tuning
fine_tuning_method: SFT
dataset: Existing Project Risk Management (PRM) course training materials converted into prompt-input-output triples
output_format: adaptive tutoring responses / personalized learning content
evaluation_method: human
educational_level: professional
key_findings: A triples-based (prompt-input-output) fine-tuning methodology reusing existing training materials improves LLM instruction-following and contextual understanding in ITS/ALP settings. The approach enables multi-dimensional personalization (project-wise and standard-wise) while highlighting challenges around data quality, overfitting, and prompt engineering complexity.

title: Enhancing Reading Comprehension: A Comparative Study of Models for Generating Questions in Children's Storybooks
venue: APIT - Asia Pac. Inf. Technol. Conf.
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: (CI1) published in 2025 at APIT 2025; (CI2) in English; (CI3) conference paper (ACM); (CI4) uses LLMs to generate reading comprehension questions from children's stories, a direct educational application; (CI5) explicitly compares prompting (zero-shot, few-shot) and fine-tuning with LoRA on T5-base, Llama 2 7B, and Mistral 7B; (CI6) full text available. Not a review or survey, not in a medical domain.
adaptation_technique: Prompting (zero-shot, few-shot) and fine-tuning with LoRA (PEFT)
fine_tuning_method: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) via PEFT; T5-base with Seq2SeqTrainer; Llama 2 7B and Mistral 7B with SFTTrainer (trl library); r=16, lora_alpha=64
dataset: FairytaleQA, 10,580 question-answer pairs derived from 278 children's stories from Project Gutenberg; kindergarten to 8th grade levels
output_format: Reading comprehension questions (free text, one question per narrative section)
evaluation_method: ROUGE-L and BLEU-4 (comparison with human-written questions)
educational_level: Primary / early childhood education (kindergarten to 8th grade)
key_findings: Fine-tuning with LoRA outperforms zero-shot and few-shot prompting in question quality. The best model is fine-tuned Mistral 7B: ROUGE-L=0.58, BLEU-4=0.39. T5-base is the fastest but produces lower-quality questions. Prompting is simpler to deploy but yields inferior results. PEFT with LoRA reduces computational costs compared to full fine-tuning.

title: An Adaptable Client-Server Architecture for Generating Educational Content using Large Language Models
venue: BULLETIN OF THE TECHNICAL COMMITTEE ON LEARNING TECHNOLOGY
justification: Journal article (2025) in English presenting a client-server architecture for automated educational content generation using LLMs (OpenAI GPT models). Directly addresses CI4 (LLM for didactic content) and CI5 (RAG + prompt engineering). The system generates MCQs, instructional materials, and adaptive learning resources, aligning with RQ2, RQ3, RQ5, RQ6, and RQ7. Full text available.
llm_base: OpenAI GPT (gpt-4o-mini mentioned explicitly); LangChain-orchestrated multi-provider support (Anthropic Claude, Mistral, Vertex AI, AWS)
adaptation_technique: RAG + prompt engineering (structured prompt templates with QVC, zero-shot / one-shot / multi-shot prompting, critic-based iterative refinement, human-AI interaction loop)
dataset: Instructor-uploaded course materials (textbooks, lecture notes, supplementary files) embedded in MongoDB Atlas Vector Search; structured course models (courses, units, learning objectives)
output_format: MCQs (standard, fill-in-the-blank, sentence-completion, true/false), instructional explanations, tutorials, concept explanations, formative assessments, formatted as JSON
evaluation_method: Research use-case studies (QuizGen, QuizMaster, EMGen): expert subject-matter rating of question quality; preliminary comparison of zero-shot vs. two-round critic vs. multi-round iterative prompting strategies; no statistically significant differences found in preliminary results; human instructor validation interface (HAI)
educational_level: Higher education (university-level courses; Athabasca University context); system designed for any educational level
key_findings: A modular, adaptable client-server architecture (Next.js + Node.js + MongoDB + LangChain) integrates RAG and structured prompt engineering to generate pedagogically aligned educational content (MCQs, explanations, assessments). Three research applications (QuizGen, QuizMaster, EMGen) demonstrate the framework. Structured QVC-driven prompting with iterative critic refinement improves alignment with learning objectives; preliminary results show expert-rated quality is favorable but no statistically significant quality differences across prompting strategies. The system supports personalization via student models and adaptive frameworks, enabling instructors to iteratively refine AI-generated materials. Future work targets RLHF, knowledge graphs, and domain-specific fine-tuning.

title: QUIZ-TUBE: ENHANCING VIDEO-BASED LEARNING WITH AUTOMATED AI QUIZ GENERATION
venue: Journal of Engineering and Technology for Industrial Applications
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2025 (CI1), in English (CI2), peer-reviewed journal article in ITEGAM-JETIA (CI3), applies an LLM (Gemma-9B) to generate interactive didactic material, specifically MCQ quizzes, from YouTube educational videos (CI4), employs fine-tuning (LoRA/PEFT on Gemma-9B) and RAG as adaptation techniques (CI5), and full text is available (CI6). Not a systematic review, not an editorial, not in a medical domain.
adaptation_technique: Fine-tuning (LoRA / PEFT) + RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) applied to Gemma-9B on a dataset of 1,000 educational video transcripts
fine_tuning_method: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) within the PEFT framework; ReFT (Representation Fine-Tuning / LoReFT) also explored
dataset: 1,000 transcripts from YouTube educational videos, augmented with synthetic question-answer pairs generated with Gemini-1.5-flash
output_format: Multiple-choice questions (MCQ) with three difficulty levels (easy, medium, hard) in interactive web quiz format
evaluation_method: BLEU score, ROUGE-L, and Accuracy (pre/post fine-tuning comparison): BLEU 45.3 to 68.7, ROUGE-L 57.2 to 74.8, Accuracy 64.5% to 81.9%
educational_level: General / multi-level (platform designed for online education accessible across different educational levels)
key_findings: Quiz-Tube converts YouTube educational videos into interactive MCQ quizzes through a pipeline that extracts transcripts (YouTube Transcript API or ASR), builds a knowledge graph (TF-IDF, NER, entity linking), and generates questions with Gemma-9B fine-tuned with LoRA. Fine-tuning on 1,000 educational transcripts improves BLEU from 45.3 to 68.7 and accuracy from 64.5% to 81.9%. The system adapts quiz difficulty to the user's performance history (adaptive learning mechanism) and allows personalization by number of questions, difficulty level, and topic focus.

title: Fine-Tuning Lightweight LLMs With Human-Curated Data on Electrical Circuit Fundamentals for E-Learning
venue: Computer Applications in Engineering Education
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2026 (CI1), in English (CI2), journal article in Computer Applications in Engineering Education (CI3), applies LLMs to didactic content generation for e-learning in electrical engineering (CI4), uses PEFT/QLoLA as the adaptation technique with a human-curated dataset (CI5), and full text is available (CI6). No exclusion criterion applies: not an editorial or review/survey, not in a medical domain, and LLMs are the core of the work.
llm_base: Meta Llama 3.2 1B; Meta Llama 3.1 8B; GPT-4.5 (commercial reference)
adaptation_technique: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) via QLoRA (Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation); bilingual (Portuguese-English) question-answer dataset on electrical circuit fundamentals, manually curated from official course materials and distilled with GPT-4o
fine_tuning_method: QLoRA (Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation) with 4-bit NormalFloat quantization (NF4) on a consumer-grade GPU (RTX 3090); full training pipeline completed in under 7 minutes
dataset: Bilingual (PT-EN) dataset curated by humans from official pedagogical materials of the FEELE course (Fundamentals of Electrotechnical Engineering, ISEP Porto); QA pairs generated with GPT-4o with a pedagogical focus (error taxonomy based on 260 real student exams)
output_format: Text answers to circuit analysis questions (QA tutoring); pedagogically structured free-text format
evaluation_method: Blind study with 77 first-year engineering students evaluating answers on a 5-point Likert scale (5 dimensions: correctness, clarity, learning value, relevance, writing style); cross-evaluation by 12 senior instructors; comparison against GPT-4.5 as SOTA baseline
educational_level: First-year undergraduate education (Electrical and Computer Engineering, ECE)
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_findings: Llama models fine-tuned with QLoRA (1B and 8B) score within one Likert point of the commercial model GPT-4.5 across the 5 quality dimensions evaluated by students; the 8B model is consistently preferred over the non-fine-tuned versions in clarity and task coverage; the full pipeline (data + training + inference) is feasible on low-cost consumer hardware (RTX 3090 GPU), demonstrating that lightweight open-source LLMs fine-tuned on human-curated data can replace costly commercial solutions in engineering e-learning tutoring.

title: Advancing AI in Higher Education: A Comparative Study of Large Language Model-Based Agents for Exam Question Generation, Improvement, and Evaluation
venue: Algorithms
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2025 (CI1), in English (CI2), journal article in Algorithms/MDPI (CI3), uses LLMs for educational tasks of exam question generation and evaluation in higher education (CI4), explicitly applies supervised fine-tuning (Meta-Llama-3.1-8B), VectorRAG, and VectorGraphRAG as adaptation techniques (CI5), and full text is available (CI6). Not a survey or systematic review but an original comparative empirical study. Does not cover a medical domain and is not an editorial.
adaptation_technique: Supervised fine-tuning on a corpus of teaching materials from university courses; vector RAG (VectorRAG with Qdrant); knowledge-graph RAG (VectorGraphRAG with Neo4j and mGTE embeddings); structured multi-step and single-step prompting for the agents
fine_tuning_method: Supervised fine-tuning of Meta-Llama-3.1-8B on a dataset distilled from 4 university courses (vlada22/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-finki-edu-5c and finki-edu-4courses, available on HuggingFace)
dataset: Curated teaching materials (lecture notes, summaries, slides) from 4 university courses at the Faculty of Computer Science of Ss. Cyril and Methodius University (Skopje): 2 theory-oriented and 2 application-oriented; bank of 1,803 exam questions balanced by category (ABQ, CEQ, KBQ) and design (single-step, multi-step); 100 new questions for validation under real conditions
output_format: Free-text exam questions (open-ended and extended-response), with numerical alignment, clarity, difficulty, and originality scores plus textual justifications (dual grading system)
evaluation_method: Automated meta-evaluator (LLM) supervised by human experts (Meta_auto + Meta_human); mixed-effects regression models to quantify agent performance; bias analysis (over/under-grading); real-world validation with 100 new questions comparing agent scores against human expert ratings (p ≈ 0.12)
educational_level: Higher education (undergraduate); computer science and engineering courses (data warehousing, operating systems, web design, statistics)
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_findings: No agent is universally optimal: the fine-tuned LLM excels at multi-step theoretical tasks requiring deep reasoning; VectorRAG outperforms on applied tasks with concrete references; VectorGraphRAG is more effective in multi-topic or multi-hop contexts. The dual scoring system (numerical scores plus textual justifications) improves bias detection and transparency. The fine-tuned LLM systematically overestimates its own performance by +1 to +1.5 points relative to the human ground truth. Validation with 100 new real questions confirms that a well-calibrated framework does not diverge significantly from expert assessment. Partial human supervision remains essential for high-stakes scenarios.

title: Design, Development, and Evaluation of an Intelligent Generation System for Contextualized Primary School Science Test Questions
venue: Int. Conf. Educ. Technol. Manag., ICETM
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2025 (CI1), in English (CI2), IEEE conference article (CI3), applies LLMs (GPT-4o + Kling AI 2.1) to generate contextualized science didactic material for primary education (CI4), uses dual-channel RAG and structured BRTE prompting with an iterative generate-evaluate-refine loop (CI5), full text available (CI6). Not an editorial, systematic review, or medical-scope article.
adaptation_technique: Dual-Channel RAG (unstructured context + structured scientific knowledge) + Structured Prompting (BRTE: Background-Role-Task-Example) + Iterative Generate-Evaluate-Refine loop + Chain of Thought (CoT)
dataset: Textbooks from People's Education Press and Daxiang Press (grades 3-6), manually compiled knowledge framework, contextualized teaching cases and sample questions, dataset built for dual-channel RAG knowledge base
output_format: Multimodal contextualized test questions (plain text, text+images, text+tables, text+images+tables) for primary school science
evaluation_method: Expert blind evaluation (3 experienced researchers) using a 4-dimension 13-indicator rubric (Scenario Quality, Question Quality, Knowledge & Skill Depth, Inquiry Level), 5-point Likert scale, Cronbach's Alpha reliability (α=0.936), ANOVA comparison vs. regional exam questions and PISA/TIMSS international assessments
educational_level: Primary school (grades 3-6, ~ages 8-12), science subject
key_findings: SciQ-Gen, the proposed system, generated contextualized science test questions that significantly outperformed regional exam questions on Knowledge & Skill Depth (M=8.98) and Inquiry Level (M=11.69), and reached near-parity with international PISA/TIMSS assessments on Scenario Quality and Question Quality. The dual-channel RAG architecture effectively separates contextual richness from scientific rigor. The iterative generate-evaluate-refine loop substantially improves output quality. The 4-dimension evaluation framework (Cronbach's α=0.936) provides a validated, replicable standard for automated educational content quality assessment.

title: A Contextualized Learner-Profiling Transformer Architecture for Adaptive Grammar Error Diagnosis and Instruction
venue: International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications
justification: Journal article (2026, IJACSA) in English that proposes AMT-T5, a fine-tuned T5 transformer framework for adaptive grammar error correction, error-type classification, and personalized feedback generation in ESL education. Directly uses supervised fine-tuning (multitask) plus DPO alignment on an LLM (T5) to produce learner-centered didactic feedback, with explicit learner profiling and personalization. Meets CI1-CI6 fully.
adaptation_technique: Supervised fine-tuning (multitask learning) + Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) + Knowledge Distillation; adaptive attention reweighting conditioned on dynamic learner error profiles
fine_tuning_method: Multitask supervised fine-tuning with joint grammar correction, error-type classification, and explanation generation losses; DPO for pedagogical alignment; Knowledge Distillation for deployment efficiency
dataset: Lang-8 Learner Corpus (Kaggle), authentic ESL learner sentences with native-speaker corrections; 80/10/10 train/val/test split
output_format: Corrected sentence + error-type label + natural language explanation/feedback (text output, three parallel decoder heads)
evaluation_method: BLEU (78.9), GLEU (90.7), full-sentence accuracy (75.3%), Levenshtein edit distance (1.7 avg), Error Reduction Rate (91.2%); ablation study; qualitative user evaluation (N=60 ESL learners)
educational_level: Adult ESL learners (language learning / L2 English instruction, not domain-specific academic level stated)
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_findings: AMT-T5 outperforms GECToR, BART-GEC, and RoBERTa-GEC by 18-22% on GLEU and ERR. Learner-conditioned adaptive attention reweighting significantly reduces recurring grammatical errors across sessions. DPO alignment improves pedagogical validity of corrections. 93% of users valued explanation-based feedback; 83% reported learning from corrections. The framework demonstrates that integrating learner profiling into the transformer attention mechanism, rather than as post-hoc processing, is the key architectural novelty for personalized educational NLP.

title: Developing a multi-layer agent framework to enhance AI-generated educational questions for cybersecurity
venue: Journal of Umm Al-Qura University for Engineering and Architecture
justification: Journal article (2025, English) that uses ChatGPT via API with domain-specific fine-tuning and a multi-layer agent framework to generate and iteratively refine educational MCQs for cybersecurity certification (CompTIA Security+). Directly addresses LLM adaptation (domain fine-tuning, scenario enrichment, adaptive feedback loops) for didactic content generation and evaluates quality via Bloom's Taxonomy. Meets all inclusion criteria CI1-CI5; no exclusion criterion applies.
adaptation_technique: Domain-specific fine-tuning + multi-layer agent framework with scenario enrichment, iterative adaptive feedback loops, and hybrid automated/expert evaluation
fine_tuning_method: Domain-specific fine-tuning with cybersecurity scenario-rich datasets; iterative refinement via multi-stage evaluation pipeline aligned to Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels
dataset: CompTIA Security+ Study Guide (Exam SY0-701): 340 book-based questions across 17 chapters used as reference/ground-truth for comparison
output_format: Multiple-choice questions (MCQs) for cybersecurity certification assessment, delivered via web platform
evaluation_method: Bloom's Taxonomy weighted scoring (100-point system across 6 cognitive levels); quantitative comparison of AI-generated vs. human-authored book questions; hybrid automated + subject-matter-expert review
educational_level: Higher education / professional certification (CompTIA Security+ SY0-701)
rq_coverage: RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7; RQ8
key_findings: AI-generated questions (ChatGPT) excel at lower-order cognitive tasks (remembering, understanding) but underperform at higher-order levels (applying, analyzing, evaluating, creating) compared to human-authored questions (74.5/100 vs. 88/100). A multi-layer agent framework with domain fine-tuning, scenario enrichment, difficulty progression, and iterative feedback loops significantly improved cognitive depth and exam relevance. The hybrid human-AI evaluation model ensured scalability and quality. Identified gaps: limited real-world scenario depth, need for reinforcement learning integration, and domain-specific dataset scarcity.

title: AI-enhanced structured dataset building for generating meaningful knowledge relationships and diversified questions
venue: Expert Systems with Applications
justification: Journal article (Expert Systems With Applications, 2026) in English meeting all inclusion criteria. Uses LLMs (GPT-4) with prompting techniques (ToT, CoT, RAG) to generate structured educational datasets and diversified Q&A pairs for a university-level database course. Directly addresses LLM adaptation for didactic content generation, multi-format output (MCQ with difficulty levels, MCQ with cognitive development levels, gap-filling), and personalization via Bloom's taxonomy alignment. No medical content; not a review/survey. It presents an original system (SKQA) with empirical evaluation.
adaptation_technique: Prompting (Tree-of-Thought, Chain-of-Thought, RAG); no fine-tuning applied; LLMs used via prompt engineering and retrieval augmentation
dataset: DBE-KT22 (knowledge tracing dataset) pre-processed into structured knowledge component (KC) relationship files; plus recognized database textbook (Elmasri, 2015) used as RAG source. Generated output: 1,017 Q&A pairs.
output_format: Multiple-choice questions (MCQ) with varying difficulty levels (basic/intermediate/advanced), multiple-choice with Bloom's cognitive development levels, and gap-filling questions, all grounded in multi-KC relationship structures
evaluation_method: Quantitative: comparison against baseline RAG models (Mistral-7B+RAG, LLaMA-3-8B+RAG, GPT-4+RAG) on Q&A quality metrics. Qualitative: human expert evaluation (20 experts, 3 universities) using 24-item questionnaire measuring relevancy, language fluency, comprehensibility, linguistic complexity, completeness, difficulty quality, cognitive development quality, usability, and overall quality (Likert 1-5); Cronbach's alpha = 0.933. Also compared against KT datasets (ASSISTments, KDCup, EdNet, DBE-KT22) and open-source FDS Q&A datasets.
educational_level: Higher education (university-level database course: Fundamentals of Database Systems); framework claimed applicable across subjects/courses
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_findings: SKQA system uses ToT-based prompting to automatically build structured knowledge component (KC) groups with multiple inter-KC relationships (3-4 KCs per question), overcoming the single-KC limitation of prior datasets. Combined with RAG and LangChain, it generates 1,017 diverse Q&A pairs covering difficulty levels (QDL) and cognitive development levels (QCD, aligned with Bloom's taxonomy) across three question types. Expert evaluation scores range 4.1-4.7/5; SKQA outperforms all three RAG baseline models. The system is general-purpose and demonstrated on a DB course but extensible to other domains (e.g., Operating Systems). Main limitation: hallucination mitigation via RAG but no fine-tuning performed.

title: Enhancing LLM with CA Domain Knowledge using Fine Tuning and RAG Techniques
venue: Proc. - Int. Conf. Adv. Syst. Emergent Technol., IC_ASET
justification: The article develops a Q&A system based on LLaMA-2 7b (fine-tuning with LoRA and multiple RAG variants) to answer Chartered Accountancy (CA) exam questions. The output consists of contextual answers to exam queries, not generation of didactic material (lessons, structured exercises, summaries, quizzes, multi-format content for teaching). It fits exactly the out-of-scope case defined: an LLM adapted to a domain without didactic material generation, oriented toward professional exam preparation Q&A rather than teaching or educational content production.
adaptation_technique: fine-tuning; LoRA; RAG; Simple RAG; Self-RAG; RAG Fusion; HyDE
fine_tuning_method: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) with PEFT on a CA Q&A dataset
dataset: Curated dataset of ICAI materials (CA Foundation syllabus): textbooks and CA exam Q&A (100 MCQ per topic from past ICAI exams)
output_format: textual answers to exam questions (Q&A)
evaluation_method: cosine similarity; SBERT similarity; ROUGE-L1; ROUGE-L2; LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4o-mini) on relevance/consistency/fluency/coherence
educational_level: Professional exam preparation (Chartered Accountancy, India)
key_findings: HyDE combined with a fine-tuned LLM (LoRA) achieves the best performance across all RAG methods evaluated. Fine-tuning improves all RAG approaches except Self-RAG. RAG Fusion underperforms in the fine-tuned model. The ft+HyDE combination maximizes semantic relevance and coherence according to NLP metrics and LLM-as-a-judge evaluation.

title: BegoniaGPT: Cultivating the large language model to be an exceptional K-12 English teacher
venue: Neural Networks
justification: Journal article (Neural Networks, 2025) proposing BegoniaGPT, a specialized LLM for K-12 English language teaching. Meets CI1 (2025), CI2 (English), CI3 (journal article), CI4 (LLM for education), CI5 (supervised fine-tuning + RLHF + RAG), CI6 (full text available). Not a review or editorial. The domain is language education, not medical. Generates personalized didactic content (exercises, lesson plans, essay correction) adapted to the educational level (primary, secondary, upper secondary).
adaptation_technique: Continued pre-training (CPT) + Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) + Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) + Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
fine_tuning_method: Continued pre-training with MLM loss on the EngCorpus (~300 MB, ~50 M tokens); SFT on 65,000 curated instructions/conversations (EngCorpus: BasCorpus + ProCorpus + VisCorpus + EmoCorpus); alignment with RLHF (reward model trained with feedback from expert teachers)
dataset: EngCorpus: proprietary corpus of ~65,000 instruction/conversation samples for K-12 English, composed of BasCorpus (10k instructions from 15 English textbooks), ProCorpus (15k exercises from 30 textbooks grades 1-12 + 12k pedagogical conversations generated with GPT-4o), VisCorpus (10k instructions from 50 newspapers/magazines/forums), EmoCorpus (30k psychological counseling conversations from an open-source dataset)
output_format: Multi-turn conversational responses: grammatical explanations, gap-fill exercises, sample essays, writing correction with detailed feedback, lesson plans, student progress tracking, emotional support
evaluation_method: Automatic benchmarks on 4 real K-12 English exams (E-EVAL 2023-2024 EEM, EEH, Eng-Gaokao 2024); automatic evaluation with Claude 3-opus; manual evaluation by experts (interactivity, heuristics, personalization, overall satisfaction); comparison against 10 SOTA LLMs (GPT-4o, ChatGPT, LLaMA 3, Qwen 2, Baichuan 4, ChatGLM 3, TechGPT, EduChat, iFlytek Spark)
educational_level: K-12 (primary, secondary, and upper secondary); specifically designed to distinguish the cognitive and curricular needs of the three levels
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_findings: BegoniaGPT outperforms 10 SOTA LLMs on 4 K-12 English benchmarks (average margin 2.86%). The full system integrates CPT + SFT + RLHF + RAG to cover three roles (student, teacher, parent). Personalization by educational level (primary focused on playful interaction, secondary on grammatical knowledge, upper secondary on critical thinking) significantly improves teacher satisfaction. Average expert evaluation is 14.81% higher than other LLMs in overall satisfaction. BegoniaGPT 2.0 (under development) will extend to university education and multiple disciplines.

title: High-Quality Distractors Generation for Human Exam Based on Reinforcement Learning from Preference Feedback
venue: NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING AND CHINESE COMPUTING, PT IV, NLPCC 2024
justification: The paper proposes DGRL, a method that fine-tunes large language models (via SFT then reinforcement learning from preference feedback) to generate high-quality distractors for multiple-choice reading comprehension questions in real-world human exams. It meets CI4 (LLMs for generating educational material, distractor generation is a core component of exam/quiz creation), CI5 (applies fine-tuning: SFT + RL), and CI1-CI3 (2025 conference paper in English). The educational context is explicit: generating exam question components for English reading comprehension assessments.
llm_base: LLaMA; ChatGLM; ChatGPT (GPT-4, used for reward model ranking)
adaptation_technique: fine-tuning
fine_tuning_method: SFT + RLHF
dataset: EQG-RACE dataset (English reading comprehension questions with human-written distractors); ~1000 samples ranked for reward model training (~66,000 pairwise comparisons)
output_format: quiz (multiple-choice question distractors)
evaluation_method: mixed (automatic metrics: BLEU, ROUGE-L, ACD, Self-BLEU, D-1, D-2, BERT accuracy; human evaluation by experienced English examiners)
educational_level: higher ed / language learning (English reading comprehension exams)
key_findings: DGRL, combining supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning from preference feedback, outperforms baselines in generating high-quality, misleading, and diverse distractors for real-world English reading comprehension exams. DGRL_RL^Q achieves the highest proportion of strongly misleading distractors (72.35%) and lowest BERT answering accuracy (46.80%), confirming that RL-based training produces more effective exam distractors than SFT alone.

title: Optimizing Diary Studies Learning Outcomes with Fine-Tuned Large Language Models on the Diaryquest Platform
venue: Proc. Front. Educ. Conf. FIE
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2025 (CI1), in English (CI2), IEEE FIE conference article (CI3), uses a fine-tuned LLM to generate personalized didactic feedback in a university educational context (CI4), applies fine-tuning with LoRA on Mistral 7B (CI5), and full text is available (CI6). No exclusion criteria apply: not an editorial, not a review/survey, not medical in scope, and explicitly uses an LLM.
adaptation_technique: Supervised fine-tuning with LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) on Mistral 7B; knowledge distillation using a teacher model of more than 70B parameters to generate the training data
fine_tuning_method: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation), modifying attention matrices W_q and W_v; 13,222,350 training tokens, 10 epochs, learning rate 0.0001; dataset generated via distillation from a teacher model >70B
dataset: 1,046 diary entries selected from 1,372 entries collected in the introductory HCI course at Virginia Tech (multiple semesters); System/User/Assistant triad structure with feedback and summaries generated by the teacher model
output_format: Free text: personalized feedback per diary entry and thematic summaries; additional output in JSON for the semantic graph (topics and relations)
evaluation_method: Survey with 12 participants (10 students, 2 instructors): 5-point Likert scale for relevance, clarity, depth, and usefulness of feedback; UX evaluation of the system (functionality, intuitiveness, thematic analysis, semantic search); model performance comparison by input length (detailed >500 words, general 200-300, brief ~100 words)
educational_level: University higher education (introductory HCI course, Computer Science, Virginia Tech)
key_findings: The Mistral 7B model fine-tuned with LoRA generates high-quality feedback for student diary entries in HCI courses, outperforming other open-source models (Llama 3.1 8B, Gemma 2 9B) in blind rater evaluation (Borda count). LLM feedback significantly improves the depth of student reflection and teaching efficiency in large-enrollment courses. The model performs better on short and medium-length entries (Avg 4.00-4.33/5) than on detailed entries over 500 words (Avg 3.92/5). The DiaryQuest platform with integrated LLM achieves an overall satisfaction score of 4.58/5. The evaluation with only 12 participants limits generalizability of results.

title: Tailored Tales: Enhancing Children's Reading Comprehension with Preference-Tuned Automatic Story Generation
venue: Proc. - Int. Conf. Comput. Artif. Intell., ICCAI
justification: The article applies DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) and KTO (Kahneman-Tversky Optimization) on Mistral-7B to generate short stories aimed at reading comprehension for primary school children (EGRA). It meets CI1 (central LLM: Mistral-7B), CI2 (explicit educational context), CI3 (experimental methodology with annotated datasets and metrics), CI4 (generation of narrative didactic material), CI5 (published 2025, ICCAI 2025), and CI6 (accessible via IEEE Xplore with USAL license). It is not an editorial (CE1), not a survey (CE2), not biomedical (CE3), and the LLM plays a central role in direct didactic generation (not CE5/out-of-scope).
adaptation_technique: DPO (Direct Preference Optimization); KTO (Kahneman-Tversky Optimization); zero-shot prompting
fine_tuning_method: Preference-based fine-tuning: DPO with good/bad story pairs from the HANNA dataset; KTO with the ROCStories dataset and binary classification of EGRA criteria using Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2
dataset: HANNA (Human-Annotated Narratives for Automatic Story Generation, 1056 stories, 96 prompts); ROCStories (100,000 commonsense stories of 5 sentences, Amazon Mechanical Turk)
output_format: short stories (brief narrative, ~60 words); narrative text in English for children
evaluation_method: manual evaluation by a human expert using the EGRA rubric (16 criteria); readability metrics: Flesch-Kincaid, Flesch Ease, Coleman Liau, Dale Chall, ARI, SPACHE, Linsear Write; semantic diversity: CLIP encoding (cosine similarity), silhouette score, BERT score; N-gram diversity (Distinct-N, bigrams)
educational_level: Early primary education (early grades, children learning to read, EGRA target audience)
rq_coverage: RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_findings: Preference tuning (DPO and KTO) on Mistral-7B significantly improves the quality and diversity of generated stories compared to zero-shot GPT-4, while preserving narrative coherence and suitability for a child reader profile. DPO achieves 89% EGRA compliance (adjusted) and KTO achieves 87%. Diversity measured with BERT score and N-gram is comparable to or better than classical inspiration-based methods. KTO produces greater lexical diversity while DPO shows greater semantic uniformity. The work demonstrates that preference tuning is an efficient alternative to full fine-tuning for generating didactic material personalized by pedagogical criteria.

title: Book2QA: A Framework for Integrating LLMs to Generate High-quality QA Data from Textbooks
venue: Proc Int Jt Conf Neural Networks
justification: Paper proposes Book2QA, a multi-LLM framework that generates high-quality QA datasets from textbooks using hierarchical prompting grounded in Bloom's taxonomy, then fine-tunes smaller educational chatbots (Baichuan-7B) via SFT+LoRA on the synthetic data. It also incorporates RAG for retrieval-augmented QA answering. All core inclusion criteria are met: 2025 IJCNN conference paper, English, empirical framework with fine-tuning + prompting + RAG, directly targeting educational QA generation from didactic content.
llm_base: Baichuan-7B-Chat; Baichuan-13B-Chat; InternLM-7B-Chat; Qwen-7B-Chat; GPT-4 (evaluator/baseline); Qwen-Max (baseline)
adaptation_technique: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with LoRA; hierarchical prompting based on Bloom's taxonomy (six cognitive levels); RAG with bge-base-zh + Faiss for retrieval-augmented QA
fine_tuning_method: SFT with LoRA (lora_rank=8, lora_alpha=16, lr=5e-5, 1 epoch, LlamaFactory, NVIDIA A100)
dataset: Book2QA_DI (5940 synthetic QA pairs from two computer science textbooks); Book2QA_ChineseSquad (1440 pairs); Book2QA_CMRC2018 (1440 pairs); evaluation sets DI80, DI240, ChineseSquad_eval (296 questions), CMRC2018_eval (492 questions)
output_format: QA pairs (open-ended questions + long-form answers) from textbook content
evaluation_method: Automatic: BERTScore (BF1), ROUGE-L, K-Precision; Human: 3 native-speaker evaluators rating relevance, accuracy, helpfulness on 5-point Likert scale; GPT-4 as judge with win-tie-loss comparison; Krippendorff's alpha for inter-rater agreement
educational_level: Higher education / undergraduate (computer science first-year Chinese university students)
key_findings: Book2QA generates cost-effective synthetic QA data from textbooks by integrating multiple medium-scale LLMs with Bloom's taxonomy prompting and IFD/r-IFD filtering. Models fine-tuned on Book2QA_DI outperform RAG-only versions in multi-paragraph tasks and rival SOTA models (GPT-4-RAG, Qwen-Max) at a fraction of cost ($0 local inference vs. $2/1M tokens for ChatGPT). Synthetic data exceeds human-annotated data on BF1 and ROUGE-L. The framework generalises beyond textbooks to QA datasets in other domains (ChineseSquad, CMRC2018).

title: Designing and Evaluating a 5E-Structured GenAI Coach for Guided Inquiry: A Pedagogy-to-Prompt Engineering Framework
venue: EDUCATION SCIENCES
justification: Journal article (2026, EN) presenting an original quasi-experimental empirical study (N=60, middle school). Uses GPT-4 as the foundation LLM and structured prompt engineering (5E Learning Cycle + 5S Prompting Principles translated into system prompts) as the core adaptation technique to create a pedagogically structured GenAI coach for chemistry education. Meets all inclusion criteria: CI1 (2026), CI2 (EN), CI3 (journal article), CI4 (LLM for didactic material generation and tutoring), CI5 (prompt engineering as explicit adaptation technique), CI6 (full text available). No exclusion criteria apply: not a review/survey, not medical, uses LLM (GPT-4).
adaptation_technique: Prompt engineering, 5S Prompting Principles (Set the Scene, Be Specific, Simplify Language, Structure Output, Share Feedback) combined with 5E Learning Cycle (Engage, Explore, Explain, Elaborate, Evaluate) as instructional architecture embedded in system prompts; no-code custom GPT built via OpenAI platform
dataset: Domain-specific knowledge base: content knowledge on balancing chemical equations (middle school, 8th grade) and a common error analysis table with problem types, correct answers, student errors, causes, misconceptions, and correction strategies; interaction logs from N=60 students in quasi-experimental study
output_format: Conversational dialogue (Socratic questioning, scaffolded hints, formative feedback on student answers, step-by-step guided inquiry through 5E phases)
evaluation_method: Quasi-experimental design (pre/post test, N=60, GenAI vs. control group); t-test (t(58)=2.646, p=0.011, Cohen's d=0.68); Johnson-Neyman analysis for moderation by prior knowledge; Lag Sequential Analysis (LSA) of interaction logs to verify adherence to 5E pedagogical sequence (z=11.157 for Engage→Explore transition)
educational_level: Middle school (8th grade, ~13-14 years old)
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_findings: A GPT-4 custom coach structured via a Pedagogy-to-Prompt Engineering Framework (5E Learning Cycle + 5S Prompting Principles) significantly improved middle school students' ability to balance chemical equations compared to traditional instruction (Cohen's d=0.68); the coach was particularly effective for low-prior-knowledge students (J-N threshold: pre-test score < 39.39), narrowing the achievement gap; LSA confirmed the dialogue adhered to the intended 5E pedagogical sequence; the framework was subsequently deployed at national scale on Taiwan's Adaptive Learning Platform (TALPer) using GPT-4o and Gemini 2.5 Flash, serving students from Grade 1 to 12.

title: From Uniform to Unique: Adaptive K-12 Assessment Using Large Language Models
venue: EXPERIMENTAL IR MEETS MULTILINGUALITY, MULTIMODALITY, AND INTERACTION, CLEF 2025
justification: Conference paper (2026) in English meeting all inclusion criteria. Presents PLA, an adaptive K-12 learning assistant powered by a fine-tuned LLaMA 3.1 8B Instruct model. Stage I fine-tunes LLaMA 3.1 on NCERT-derived MCQs (after initial generation via Gemini 1.0 Pro); Stage II performs adaptive diagnostic testing using Bayesian inference and difficulty-aware scoring. Directly addresses LLM fine-tuning for didactic material (MCQ generation), personalization per student profile (Bloom's Taxonomy levels, learner profiling), and educational quality evaluation. Not a survey/review; not medical domain.
