adaptation_technique: fine-tuning + prompting
fine_tuning_method: SFT
dataset: NCERT K-12 textbook passages (Indian curriculum); initial MCQs generated by Gemini 1.0 Pro, then human-validated and used to fine-tune LLaMA 3.1 and T5; dataset available on Hugging Face
output_format: quiz (MCQs aligned to Bloom's Taxonomy levels: Remember/Understand, Apply/Analyze, Evaluate/Create)
evaluation_method: mixed (empirical pilot with ~30 students grades 8-12; difficulty-aware confidence scoring; comparison of self-assessed prior vs. system score; simulation results)
rq_coverage: RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_findings: A fine-tuned LLaMA 3.1 model combined with Bayesian probabilistic diagnosis and Bloom's Taxonomy-aligned MCQ generation outperforms single-question adaptive systems by suppressing random guessing (P_topic ≈ 0.11% chance of passing by guessing). Empirical evaluation with K-12 students showed an average deviation of −5.69% between self-assessed prior knowledge and system-generated scores, validating PLA's diagnostic accuracy and capacity to reveal latent conceptual strengths.

title: AI-Based Examination Content Creation: Evaluating Large Language Models for Question Generation
venue: Int. Conf. Intell. Methods, Syst. Appl., IMSA
justification: Conference paper (IEEE IMSA 2025) that empirically fine-tunes three state-of-the-art LLMs (LLaMA 3.2, Mistral 7B, Qwen 2.5-Max) with LoRA and 4-bit quantization for automated MCQ generation across three educational benchmark datasets (SciQ, RACE, CosmosQA). Directly addresses CI4 (LLM for didactic content generation) and CI5 (fine-tuning technique). Rigorous evaluation with BERTScore, ROUGE, F1, and Token Efficiency metrics. Not a review/survey; contains original experiments and reproducible methodology (models uploaded to Hugging Face).
adaptation_technique: Fine-tuning (LoRA + 4-bit quantization via Unsloth framework)
fine_tuning_method: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) with 4-bit quantization; AdamW 8-bit optimizer; Alpaca-style prompt formatting
dataset: SciQ (13,000 MCQs for scientific reasoning), RACE (97,000+ questions from 28,000 passages for reading comprehension), CosmosQA (35,600 commonsense reasoning questions)
output_format: Multiple-choice questions (MCQ) with four answer choices (one correct + three distractors)
evaluation_method: F1-score, ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L, BERTScore (semantic similarity), Token Efficiency, Precision, Recall
educational_level: General (benchmarks span middle school, high school, and domain-specific scientific/commonsense reasoning; no explicit learner profiling)
key_findings: Mistral 7B achieved highest F1 on SciQ (0.891) and best factual precision (0.8462), making it optimal for structured scientific MCQ generation. Qwen 2.5-Max excelled on RACE (F1=0.825) and CosmosQA (F1=0.702) due to its 32k-token context and multi-step reasoning. LLaMA 3.2 performed consistently but struggled with implicit reasoning. LoRA fine-tuning with 4-bit quantization significantly improved all models' efficiency without sacrificing quality. Future work calls for domain-specific fine-tuning and bias mitigation.

title: Fine-Tuning Language Models for Pedagogy-Aligned Lesson Plans in Cybersecurity Education
venue: International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2025 (CI1), in English (CI2), indexed journal article IJACSA (CI3), applies LLMs to generate structured didactic material (lesson plans) (CI4), uses supervised fine-tuning of GPT-3.5 with an ad hoc domain dataset (CI5), full text available (CI6). No exclusion criteria apply: it is not an editorial, not a review/survey, not in a medical context, and explicitly uses an LLM. The domain is educational cybersecurity, within the scope of the TFM on controlled generation of didactic material.
adaptation_technique: Supervised fine-tuning (OpenAI fine-tuning API) with a domain dataset built using Custom GPT, KMeans clustering (TF-IDF and SBERT), and oversampling for class balancing
fine_tuning_method: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) in chat format (.jsonl), 3 epochs, batch_size=1, learning_rate_multiplier=2; dataset of 325 balanced entries with oversampling and paraphrasing
dataset: Custom lesson plan dataset in cybersecurity: 119 original entries expanded to 325 after balancing, derived from the Diploma in Cybersecurity program at King Abdulaziz University (KAU); generated via Custom GPT and validated by 8 experts (curricularists and instructors)
output_format: Structured lesson plans with five components: course title, topic, learning objectives (revised Bloom's Taxonomy), learning theory, and teaching strategy
evaluation_method: Automatic: BLEU (73.54), ROUGE-1 (82.19), ROUGE-2 (68.91), ROUGE-L (78.16), BERTScore F1 (97.43); human evaluation by educational experts and cybersecurity instructors on 6-8 pedagogical criteria
educational_level: Higher education / professional training in cybersecurity (university diploma, King Abdulaziz University)
key_findings: Fine-tuning GPT-3.5 with a pedagogically structured dataset produces lesson plans with high lexical and semantic fidelity (BERTScore F1=97.43, BLEU=73.54). Integrating Bloom's Taxonomy, learning theories, and teaching strategies explicitly into the fine-tuning process, rather than applying them in post-processing, is key to obtaining instructionally valid outputs. The framework is transferable to other domains by redefining the knowledge base.

title: Multimodal Quiz Generation via RAG with LLM-as-Judge Evaluation
venue: Proc. Front. Educ. Conf. FIE
justification: Conference paper (IEEE FIE 2025) in English presenting a novel multimodal quiz generation framework based on RAG (multimodal retrieval with BridgeTower embeddings, LanceDB vector store, LangChain prompt construction) using LLaMA 3.1 (8B) for text generation and LLaVA for vision-language understanding. Generates pedagogically aligned MCQs from lecture videos integrating audio, visual, and textual modalities. Evaluation uses LLM-as-Judge (Claude Sonnet v3.5, GPT-4, LLaMA 3.1) compared against human expert raters with inter-rater agreement metrics (Cohen's Kappa, Krippendorff's Alpha, Spearman's Rho). Directly relevant to RQ2 (RAG), RQ4 (output format: MCQ multimodal), RQ5 (evaluation: LLM-as-Judge + human), RQ6 (quality metrics: correctness, relevance, faithfulness), and RQ8 (multimodal content generation). All inclusion criteria met; no exclusion criteria apply.
llm_base: LLaMA 3.1 (8B); LLaVA; BridgeTower; Claude Sonnet v3.5; GPT-4
adaptation_technique: RAG (multimodal: text + image retrieval via BridgeTower embeddings and LanceDB vector store) + structured prompting (LangChain dynamic prompt construction)
dataset: MIT 6.100L Introduction to Computer Science and Programming Using Python (Fall 2022) lecture videos -- first video used; 15 MCQs generated across 3 topic segments
output_format: Multiple-choice questions (MCQ) with 1 correct answer and 3 plausible distractors; multimodal input (audio transcript via Whisper + video frames via cv2 + text slides)
evaluation_method: Two-stage: (1) Retrieval evaluation, Hit Rate and Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) for text and image modalities; (2) Generation quality, Correctness, Relevance, Faithfulness (5-point Likert); LLM-as-Judge comparison (Claude Sonnet v3.5, GPT-4, LLaMA 3.1 8B) vs. 4 human expert raters using Cohen's Kappa, Krippendorff's Alpha (ordinal and nominal), Spearman's Rho, Percentage Agreement
educational_level: Higher education / undergraduate (introductory computer science course)
key_findings: The multimodal RAG pipeline achieves Hit Rate 0.91 (text) / 0.83 (image) and MRR 0.82 (text) / 0.71 (image). Generated MCQs score mean Correctness 4.028/5, Relevance 0.821, Faithfulness 0.898. LLM-as-Judge models show higher inter-model agreement (Kappa up to 0.70, Krippendorff's Alpha up to 0.87) than human-LLM agreement (Kappa 0.34-0.44), indicating LLMs are reliable proxies for surface-level quality but diverge from humans on nuanced pedagogical judgments. Claude Sonnet v3.5 best aligns with human raters (79% agreement, Kappa 0.41). LLMs tend to overestimate quality on vague or incomplete questions, underscoring the need for human oversight in pedagogically rigorous assessment.

title: Toward Generating Quality Test Questions and Answers Using Quantized Low-Rank Adapters in LLMs
venue: IEEE Access
justification: The article is a core case in the mapping: it applies QLoRA (fine-tuning with quantized low-rank adapters) on LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct to generate high-quality exam questions and answers (KCSAT-ENG). It meets all inclusion criteria: CI1 (LLM as central component: LLaMA-3-8B), CI2 (explicit educational context: didactic material generation for high school students), CI3 (empirical method with quantitative experimentation: 19 QLoRA configurations evaluated), CI4 (didactic material generation: exam questions and answers across 22 task types), CI5 (published in 2025), CI6 (accessible: IEEE Access open access article). It is not an editorial (CE1), not a survey (CE2), not biomedical (CE3), and the LLM drives the didactic generation (not out-of-scope or CE5).
adaptation_technique: QLoRA; LoRA; instruction fine-tuning; PEFT
fine_tuning_method: QLoRA (Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation): fine-tuning with 4-bit NF4 quantization and LoRA adapters; separate models for question and answer generation; Chain-of-Thought in question generation prompts
dataset: KCSAT-ENG (Korea College Scholastic Ability Test - English section): custom dataset built from 157 real KCSAT exams and mock tests (3,299 questions, 22 task types); answers generated with GPT-4o
output_format: Multiple-choice questions (KCSAT-style); answers with translation and explanatory commentary; 22 task types (fill-in-the-blank, sentence ordering, grammar, etc.)
evaluation_method: Format verification (pass rate); BERTScore; BLEU; ROUGE; METEOR; multiple-choice accuracy; professional checklist for educational adequacy
educational_level: Upper secondary education (students preparing for the KCSAT, the university entrance exam in South Korea)
key_findings: QLoRA applied to LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct improves format verification rate in question generation by 41.5% (81.8% vs 57.8% baseline) and in answer generation by 16.1% (85.7% vs 73.0%) compared to the base model without fine-tuning. The optimal configuration for questions is rank=32, alpha=8 without quantization; for answers, rank=64, alpha=16 with quantization. Using separate models for question and answer generation, combined with a post-validation regeneration step, increases format consistency and factual accuracy.

title: Designing an LLM-Based IELTS Question Generator, Assessment, and Personalized Training System: Architecture and Research Agenda
venue: Int. Conf. Electr. Eng./Electron., Comput., Telecommun. Inf. Technol., ECTI-CON
justification: NOAH is an LLM-based system that fine-tunes GPT-4o on authentic IELTS materials to generate exam questions, audio scripts, and practice content across all four IELTS skills (Listening, Reading, Writing, Speaking), satisfying all inclusion criteria. The paper proposes a concrete implementation using fine-tuning via LLMOps, prompt chaining, and RAG-adjacent retrieval, with preliminary evaluation using the METEOR metric and human expert review.
adaptation_technique: fine-tuning; prompting
fine_tuning_method: SFT
dataset: 20 authentic IELTS past papers per module (Cambridge University Press, 2020-2024), processed via OCR and formatted for GPT-4o fine-tuning
output_format: quiz; exercise; other
evaluation_method: automatic-metric; human
educational_level: language-learning
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7; RQ8
key_findings: Initial evaluations confirm that fine-tuned GPT-4o generates IELTS-standard content with high METEOR scores and positive expert reviews, though topic drift in the writing module and spatial limitations for maps and diagrams highlight areas for future improvement. The system achieves strong personalization via adaptive mock tests and skill-specific LLM pipelines, with RLHF planned for grading refinement.

title: MisstepMath: A Diverse Student Mistake Dataset for AI in Mathematics Teacher Training
venue: ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE IN EDUCATION, AIED 2025, PT I
justification: The paper's primary contribution is MisstepMath, a semi-synthetic dataset of 12,000 student math mistakes paired with teacher responses, designed to support AI-driven teacher training. Although the abstract mentions that the dataset can be used for fine-tuning conversational AI and RAG systems, these are presented as potential downstream applications, not implemented or evaluated in the paper itself. The study does not propose or assess a system that uses LLMs to generate didactic material for learners; rather, it constructs a data resource for training AI models that simulate student errors to help teachers practice. This focus on teacher preparation through student-error simulation falls outside the PICOC scope, which targets LLM-based generation of educational content (lesson plans, exercises, explanations, quizzes) directed at learners, applying FT/RAG/prompting as active methodological contributions.
llm_base: ChatGPT (GPT-4, used for data augmentation in dataset construction)
adaptation_technique: Prompting (used during dataset generation phase only, not as a study contribution)
dataset: MisstepMath -- 12,000 semi-synthetic student math mistakes (K-Grade 8) with teacher responses, categorized into 32 challenge types; seed data from 15 expert educators, expanded with ChatGPT, reviewed by experts
output_format: Structured dataset (JSON/tabular): grade, topic, sub-topic, challenge type, student mistake, teacher response
evaluation_method: Lexical diversity (TTR), semantic cosine similarity, human expert review, comparison of fine-tuned model vs. zero-shot and RAG baselines
educational_level: K-8 (Kindergarten through Grade 8), mathematics
key_findings: MisstepMath (12,000 instances) enables AI models to generate more diverse and contextually relevant simulated student interactions. A fine-tuned model using MisstepMath reduced repeated student mistakes to 21% and achieved TTR=0.88, outperforming zero-shot and RAG baselines. The dataset categorizes 32 error types across 9 grade levels, supporting adaptive tutoring and teacher training systems.

title: Level-specific feedback generation for scene descriptions via fine-tuning multimodal large language models
venue: Computers and Education: Artificial Intelligence
justification: Journal article (2025, Computers and Education: Artificial Intelligence) in English. Uses fine-tuning (LoRA) of a multimodal LLM (Qwen-2 + ViT backbone, LLaVA architecture) combined with RAG for level-specific feedback generation in an English language learning context (EFL scene description tasks, K-12 Hong Kong curriculum). Directly addresses CI4 (LLM for didactic content generation), CI5 (fine-tuning + RAG), and provides strong educational personalization aligned to learner proficiency levels (key stages 1-4). Full text available.
adaptation_technique: Supervised fine-tuning with LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) on a curriculum-aligned multimodal instruction-following dataset; RAG used for dataset construction (retrieval of curriculum word lists via vector DB) and at inference for lexical control
fine_tuning_method: LoRA (rank=128, alpha=256) applied to both the language model backbone (Qwen-2) and the vision-language connector (MLP); visual instruction tuning paradigm; 1 epoch, batch size 16, cosine LR decay
dataset: Custom curriculum-aligned multimodal dataset of 6,200 unique image-sentence-feedback triplets, constructed with GPT-4o + RAG using Hong Kong primary and secondary English word lists (key stages 1-4); covers topics: animals, food, home, people, school life, sports, health, city
output_format: Natural language written feedback (text) adapted to four educational levels (key stages 1-4) for English scene description tasks
evaluation_method: Expert human evaluation (200 samples, 2 raters) on correctness, readability, and educational effectiveness; SBERT semantic similarity; lexical complexity analysis; pilot learning-gains study comparing level-specific vs. generic feedback; comparison against LLaVA-1.6 7B, Llama 3.2-Vision 11B, GPT-4o baselines
educational_level: K-12 (primary and secondary, key stages 1-4, Hong Kong curriculum); EFL learners
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_findings: Fine-tuned MLLM significantly outperforms open-source (LLaVA, Llama) and closed-source (GPT-4o) baselines in semantic alignment and lexical appropriateness. The model achieves level-appropriate vocabulary and reduced hallucination. Pilot study shows students receiving level-specific feedback achieved greater learning gains than those receiving generic feedback. RAG-based dataset construction with curriculum word lists is a practical method for building domain-specific instruction-following data for educational fine-tuning.

title: Automatic Large Language Models Creation of Interactive Learning Lessons
justification: The paper meets all inclusion criteria: (CI1) GPT-4o is the central LLM; (CI2) the context is explicitly educational, covering lesson generation for training secondary-school mathematics tutors; (CI3) the method is empirical, with quantitative and qualitative evaluation by human raters using a rubric; (CI4) the central goal is the automatic generation of structured didactic material, specifically interactive lessons with scenarios, instruction and questions; (CI5) published in 2025 (arXiv:2506.17356); (CI6) accessible via PDF. It uses RAG and prompt engineering with subtask decomposition as adaptation techniques. It is not an editorial, survey, biomedical study, or excluded on CE5 grounds.
adaptation_technique: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation); prompt engineering; task decomposition prompting
fine_tuning_method: none; no fine-tuning is performed; the system relies entirely on RAG and prompt design with task decomposition
dataset: Research articles on effective tutoring practices (retrieved via RAG); human reference lessons created by two instructional designers (3 public lessons on OSF)
output_format: Structured interactive lesson; scenarios with multiple-choice and open-ended questions; instructional section with examples; title page with learning objectives; conclusion section
evaluation_method: Human evaluation by two instructional designers; instructional design rubric (based on lesson design research); consensus scores per section and criterion; qualitative comparative analysis with human-authored lessons
educational_level: Training of novice tutors for middle school mathematics in online environments
key_findings: Breaking generation into 3 segments produces higher-quality lessons than single-pass or 5-segment generation. The RAG-based approach with GPT-4o yields pedagogically sound, well-structured and scenario-diverse lessons, with a significant time saving over manual authoring. The main limitations are generic feedback for incorrect answers, unclear instructions, and hallucination of bibliographic references even under RAG. Human-AI collaboration is identified as the optimal workflow.

title: PromptHive: Demonstrating Collaborative, Human-Centered OER Creation with LLMs
venue: TWO DECADES OF TEL. FROM LESSONS LEARNT TO CHALLENGES AHEAD, EC-TEL 2025, PT II
justification: Conference demo paper (L@S 2025) presenting PromptHive, an open-source platform that uses LLMs (GPT-3.5/4/4o, Llama) with collaborative prompt engineering to generate high-quality OER educational hints. Meets all inclusion criteria: 2025-26, English, conference paper, LLM for didactic content generation, prompting as adaptation technique, full text available. No exclusion criterion applies: it is an empirical system paper (not a review), non-medical, and LLM-centric.
adaptation_technique: prompt engineering (human-in-the-loop collaborative prompt authoring and iterative refinement via shared prompt library)
dataset: OATutor college algebra structured hint dataset (spreadsheet-based content pool)
output_format: educational hints (scaffolded OER hints for math problems)
evaluation_method: SUS usability scale (89/100), NASA-TLX cognitive load reduction (50%), learning gains pre/post-test (358 college students via Prolific)
educational_level: higher education / undergraduate (college algebra)
key_findings: PromptHive enables subject matter experts to collaboratively author and refine LLM prompts to generate high-quality educational hints at scale. It achieved a usability score of 89/100 (SUS), reduced cognitive load by 50% and hint creation time from months to hours. Learning gains with PromptHive-generated hints were statistically equivalent to human-authored hints (no significant difference), demonstrating that human-in-the-loop prompt engineering can match expert-curated content quality while drastically improving scalability.

title: Enhancing Equitable Access to Secondary Education in Malawi Using Generative AI: A Pilot Study on the Agriculture Subject
venue: Electronic Journal of Information Systems in Developing Countries
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2026 (CI1), in English (CI2), journal article (CI3), uses a fine-tuned LLM (LLaMA 2 3B) to generate didactic material, including notes, MCQs and answers to curricular questions, in secondary education (CI4), applies supervised fine-tuning on a locally curated corpus (CI5), and the full text is available (CI6). No exclusion criterion applies: it is not an editorial, review or survey, not biomedical, and it uses an LLM explicitly.
adaptation_technique: Supervised fine-tuning on a curated corpus from the Malawi national curriculum (MSCE): digital textbooks, past exams and study guides on agriculture
fine_tuning_method: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) of the pretrained LLaMA 2 3B model on a locally curated corpus from the MSCE agriculture curriculum
dataset: Custom curated corpus: digital textbooks, past exams and study guides from the MSCE agriculture curriculum in Malawi
output_format: Conversational chatbot with: auto-generated notes, multiple-choice questionnaires (MCQs), answers to specific curriculum questions, student performance tracking
evaluation_method: Mixed-methods pilot study (quantitative and qualitative): TAM surveys (PU/PEOU), DeLone and McLean IS Success model, focus groups, system usage metrics; 200 students and 20 teachers over 6 weeks
educational_level: Secondary education (Form 3 and 4, equivalent to upper secondary) in Malawi
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_findings: The LLaMA 2 3B chatbot fine-tuned on the MSCE curriculum demonstrated technical viability in resource-constrained schools: 93% of participants said they would continue using it and would recommend it, and 82% were satisfied. Local curricular alignment was key to acceptance and reduced hallucinations. The system acted as a scaffolding tool in line with Vygotsky's ZPD, supporting autonomous learning. The ICT4D context highlights that access to electricity and internet remains a barrier to scaling.

title: Multi-objective math problem generation using large language model through an adaptive multi-level retrieval augmentation framework
venue: Information Fusion
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: (CI1) published in 2025 in Information Fusion; (CI2) in English; (CI3) journal article; (CI4) uses LLMs to generate didactic material, specifically multi-objective mathematics problems with question type, knowledge point and difficulty annotations; (CI5) employs adaptive RAG (AMRAF) as the main technique, with LoRA fine-tuning of the mentor model; (CI6) full text available. No exclusion criterion applies: it is not an editorial, review or survey, not biomedical, and it uses LLMs explicitly.
adaptation_technique: Adaptive RAG (AMRAF: Adaptive Multi-level Retrieval Augmentation Framework) combined with LoRA fine-tuning of the mentor model (Qwen1.5-14B-Chat) for conditional retrieval activation
fine_tuning_method: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) on Qwen1.5-14B-Chat as the mentor model; alpaca format for training data; AdamW optimizer, 3 epochs, A100 GPU
dataset: MMPG (Multi-objective Math Problem Generation): dataset built by the authors with 9,000 mathematics problems in Chinese (LaTeX), annotated with question type, knowledge point and difficulty; elementary to secondary levels; 16,000 question-answer pairs for mentor training (7:1 split)
output_format: Multi-objective mathematical word problems with control over: question type (true/false, multiple choice, fill-in-the-blank, problem solving, proof), knowledge point, and difficulty level (0.1-1.0)
evaluation_method: Quality score assessed by GPT-4 (LLM judge) across three dimensions: question type, knowledge point and difficulty; direct A/B comparison between models (GPT-4o as arbiter); metrics: solvability, coherence, novelty and pedagogical adequacy
educational_level: Primary and secondary education (grades 1-9, K-8/middle school); personalised teaching context for teachers and students
key_findings: AMRAF substantially improves multi-objective generation of mathematics problems with LLMs without requiring parameter adjustment of the base generative model. The LoRA-fine-tuned mentor model adaptively decides when to activate retrieval, avoiding degradation on simple queries, which occurs in 40%+ of cases with indiscriminate RAG. Multi-level retrieval combines semantic similarity search and generation-objective search with a fine-grained feature scorer. The framework is plug-and-play and compatible with both open and closed LLMs. The MMPG dataset of 9,000 samples provides a benchmark for future research on multi-objective mathematics problem generation.

title: LLM4CGDS: Large language model-based agents for Chinese graded document simplification
venue: Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence
justification: The paper proposes LLM4CGDS, an agent-based framework for Chinese Graded Document Simplification (CGDS) targeting non-native Chinese language learners. Although the motivating context is language education (graded reading), the paper's core contribution is an NLP text simplification pipeline, not the generation of didactic materials such as lesson plans, exercises, assessments or explanations. The framework uses rule-guided prompting with external knowledge retrieval (dictionaries, corpora) across four specialized agents (Character, Word, Sentence, Discourse), explicitly without fine-tuning. The PICOC of this mapping requires LLMs applied to generating or adapting educational/didactic content; text simplification for reading accessibility is a distinct NLP task that falls outside that scope.
adaptation_technique: Rule-guided prompting with retrieval-augmented knowledge (dictionaries, example corpora); no fine-tuning
dataset: JWDS (38 documents with human-crafted simplifications) and MDDS (733 documents across four domains: School, News, Novels, Wiki)
output_format: Graded simplified Chinese documents at three proficiency levels (Advanced/Intermediate/Beginner, aligned with HSK 3.0)
evaluation_method: Automatic metrics (readability control, meaning preservation) on two datasets; comparison against direct prompting of state-of-the-art LLMs; human evaluation not explicitly detailed in abstract
educational_level: Non-native Chinese language learners (all levels: beginner to advanced, aligned with HSK 1-9)
key_findings: LLM4CGDS substantially outperforms direct prompting of state-of-the-art LLMs in both readability control and meaning preservation on two Chinese document simplification datasets. The agent-based multi-granular architecture (character, word, sentence, discourse) enables explicit HSK-level control without supervised fine-tuning.

title: System Design and Evaluation of RAG-Enhanced Digital Humans in Design Education: Analyzing Cognitive Load and Instructional Efficiency
venue: Applied Sciences (Switzerland)
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: journal article (2026, English), presents an LLM-based educational system (Qwen3-30B) with RAG as the central adaptation technique, deployed locally for instruction in university design. It evaluates pedagogical impact through an RCT (N=150) measuring learning outcomes, cognitive load and instructional efficiency. It is not a review, not biomedical, and the full text is available.
llm_base: Qwen3-30B (MoE, ~3B active parameters, locally deployed via Ollama)
adaptation_technique: RAG with BGE-Large-Zh embeddings and FAISS index over a knowledge base of design history (digital textbooks, academic articles, architectural case studies); digital human interface (LiveTalking) as an audiovisual delivery channel
dataset: Internally curated knowledge base: design history textbooks, academic articles and architectural case studies from Dalian Art College; not publicly available
output_format: Real-time conversational responses delivered through an audiovisual digital human (avatar); automated lecture scripts; contextual responses to student questions
evaluation_method: Multi-centre RCT (N=150, 3 universities); post-test scores (Cohen's d=1.14); Paas Mental Effort Scale (cognitive load, d=1.71); behavioural engagement metrics (d=1.39); Instructional Efficiency analysis (E experimental=+0.72 vs. control=-0.68)
educational_level: Higher education (university students of design)
key_findings: The RAG-based digital human system significantly reduces extraneous cognitive load (d=1.71) and improves academic performance (d=1.14) and engagement (d=1.39) compared to traditional instruction. The instructional efficiency of the experimental group (E=+0.72) versus control (E=-0.68) confirms that RAG frees cognitive resources for higher-order learning. Local deployment with Qwen3-30B and Ollama ensures data privacy and feasibility in resource-limited educational settings.

title: AI-Driven Question Generation System: Benchmarking Large Language Models for Examination Systems
venue: Proc. Stud. Res. Conf. Appl. Comput.: AI Innov. a Better Tomorrow, SRC
justification: Conference paper (2025) in English that benchmarks and fine-tunes three LLMs (LLaMA 3.2, Mistral 7B, Qwen 2.5-Max) for automated MCQ generation in educational assessment contexts. Uses LoRA fine-tuning and 4-bit quantization. Directly addresses CI4 (LLM for didactic content), CI5 (fine-tuning technique), CI1 (2025), CI2 (EN), CI3 (conference paper). Evaluates quality via BERTScore, ROUGE, and Token Efficiency across three benchmark educational datasets.
adaptation_technique: Fine-tuning (LoRA + 4-bit quantization via Unsloth framework)
fine_tuning_method: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) with 4-bit quantization
dataset: SciQ (13,000+ science MCQs), RACE (97,000+ reading comprehension questions from middle/high school exams), CosmosQA (35,600 commonsense reasoning questions)
output_format: Multiple-choice questions (MCQ) with distractors
evaluation_method: BERTScore (semantic similarity), ROUGE-1/2/L (lexical overlap), Token Efficiency (fluency and informativeness), F1-score, Precision, Recall
educational_level: Secondary (middle/high school via RACE), general/higher education (SciQ, CosmosQA)
key_findings: Mistral 7B achieved highest F1 on SciQ (0.891) and best ROUGE-L (0.3699), excelling at structured scientific MCQs. Qwen 2.5-Max led on RACE (F1=0.825) and CosmosQA (F1=0.702), outperforming in multi-step and commonsense reasoning. LLaMA 3.2 struggled with implicit reasoning (lowest F1=0.646 on CosmosQA). Fine-tuned LLMs significantly improve automated assessment quality; model choice should match task type (structured vs. reasoning-heavy).

title: beSEL: A Human-Aligned LLM Teaching Assistant for Enhancing Social-Emotional Learning
venue: ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE IN EDUCATION: POSTERS AND LATE BREAKING RESULTS, WORKSHOPS AND TUTORIALS, INDUSTRY AND INNOVATION TRACKS, PRACTITIONERS, DOCTORAL CONSORTIUM, BLUE SKY, AND WIDEAIED, AIED 2025, PT II
justification: Published in 2025 as a conference paper (AIED 2025, CCIS 2591, pp. 93-101), in English, and directly addresses fine-tuning LLMs (Qwen2.5-3B with LoRA) to generate educational/didactic material for Social-Emotional Learning (SEL). The system generates lesson plans and supports classroom interaction, applying supervised fine-tuning with LoRA as the core adaptation technique, satisfying CI1-CI6.
adaptation_technique: fine-tuning
fine_tuning_method: LoRA
dataset: Two custom SEL datasets built from 362 award-winning elementary school classroom videos and lesson plans: (1) Teachers' Response QA dataset (instructional interactions) and (2) Teaching Design QA dataset (lesson plan themes and designs), constructed via multi-round LLM-based segmentation and matching.
output_format: lesson plan, classroom interaction dialogue, question answering
evaluation_method: human
rq_coverage: RQ2; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7; RQ8
key_findings: beSEL, fine-tuned with LoRA on domain-specific SEL datasets, demonstrates substantially greater emotional intelligence and situational understanding than general-purpose LLMs (e.g., GPT-4o), validating supervised fine-tuning as an effective strategy for human-aligned educational content generation. Future work plans to incorporate RLHF and multimodal support (speech) for more expressive classroom interactions.

title: TwinStar: A Novel Design for Enhanced Test Question Generation Using Dual-LLM Engine
venue: Applied Sciences (Switzerland)
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: 2025 journal article (CI1, CI3), in English (CI2), applies LLMs to generate test questions in higher education (CI4), uses fine-tuning (prompt-tuning on ChatGLM2-6B and LLaMA2-13B) and multi-round prompting as central techniques (CI5), full text available (CI6). It is not a review, survey or editorial, and contains no medical content.
adaptation_technique: Fine-tuning (prompt-tuning / P-tuning) combined with multi-round prompting; dual-LLM architecture (generation engine and cognitive evaluation engine)
fine_tuning_method: Prompt-tuning (P-tuning) on ChatGLM2-6B and LLaMA2-13B using a dataset annotated with Bloom's taxonomy levels
dataset: Public dataset of exam questions annotated with Bloom's taxonomy (Figshare: Exam_Question_Datasets/22597957); primary domain: university calculus
output_format: Open-ended test questions (exam questions) classified by Bloom's cognitive level
evaluation_method: Automatic metrics: Bloom Adherence (BA) and Knowledge Relevance (KR); comparison with GPT-3.5, GPT-4, Claude and Bard as baselines
educational_level: Higher education (university calculus)
key_findings: TwinStar, a dual engine of lightweight fine-tuned LLMs (ChatGLM2-6B), outperforms GPT-4 and Bard in adherence to Bloom's cognitive level (+50% vs Bard, +21% vs GPT-4) and in overall question quality (+12% vs Bard, +2% vs GPT-4), while consuming negligible memory. The system design combining fine-tuning with multi-round prompting is more efficient than scaling model size. The iterative cognitive evaluation between the two LLMs is the key improvement mechanism.

title: Knowledge-enhanced large language models for automatic lesson plan generation
venue: Humanities and Social Sciences Communications
justification: Journal article (2025) in English. Proposes LessonPlanLM, a knowledge-enhanced LLM framework for automatic lesson plan generation. Directly uses LLMs with both supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and retrieval-augmented fine-tuning (RAFT) to generate structured didactic materials (lesson plans). Addresses structural integrity, logical coherence, content accuracy, and knowledge-awareness, fully aligned with RQ1, RQ2, RQ3, RQ4, RQ5, RQ6, RQ7. No medical/health domain. Full text available.
adaptation_technique: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) + Retrieval-Augmented Fine-Tuning (RAFT) using a lesson plan knowledge base (LPKB) with curriculum extractor, semantics collector, and knowledge retriever
fine_tuning_method: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with a domain-specific lesson plan dataset (139,512 plans / 521,326 components); RAFT combining SFT with RAG from an external knowledge base at inference time
dataset: LessonPlanLM dataset: 139,512 lesson plans (521,326 components) crawled from Chinese third-party educational platforms plus GPT-4-simulated plans; covers K-12 (grades 1-12), including 68,363 elementary, 42,594 middle school, 28,555 high school plans
output_format: Structured lesson plan with 7 components: textbook analysis (TA), student learning analysis (SLA), lesson objectives (LO), key & difficult points (KDP), materials (MAT), lesson procedure (LP), assessment methods (AE)
evaluation_method: 4-dimensional human evaluation rubric (structure soundness, content accuracy, logic consistency, language finesse); Fleiss Kappa inter-annotator agreement; comparison against 8 LLM baselines and human-written plans from NPSSSEP and Wookey platforms; also GPT-4 automated scoring
educational_level: K-12 (elementary, middle school, high school); current focus on Chinese curriculum; framework designed to generalise internationally
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_findings: LessonPlanLM (Qwen2-72B) significantly outperforms all LLM baselines (ERNIE Bot, iFlytek Spark, GLM-4, GPT-4, Claude 3.5, Gemini 1.5, Qwen2-7B) and prior lesson-plan generation methods (Self-Critique, Prompt4ChatGPT) across all four evaluation dimensions. RAFT improves content accuracy and language finesse over SFT-only. Both semantics collector data (real S + simulated S*) and the knowledge retriever component of LPKB contribute positively to plan quality. Component-order dependency (LO+KDP then LP then MAT) is critical for logic consistency. The framework achieves performance comparable to human-written plans collected from a national smart-education platform.

title: AI-Empowered Pedagogical Reform in Power Electronics: A Practical Approach Based on RAG and Virtual Simulation
venue: Proc. Int. Symp. Artif. Intell. for Educ., ISAIE
justification: Conference paper (ACM, ISAIE 2025) presenting an empirically validated AI-enhanced pedagogical framework for Power Electronics education. Deploys a RAG-based LLM intelligent tutoring system (AITEE-PowerElec) using GPT-3.5-turbo fine-tuned on 50,000+ domain-specific Q&A pairs with FAISS vector retrieval. Includes personalized learning support, virtual simulation platform, and project-based learning module. Quasi-experimental study (n=180, 3 semesters) with control group. Meets CI1-CI6; no exclusion criteria apply.
adaptation_technique: RAG with domain-specific fine-tuning; FAISS vector database (15,000+ technical documents, 768-dim embeddings); BM25 + dense retrieval hybrid; knowledge graph integration
fine_tuning_method: Supervised fine-tuning on 50,000+ power electronics Q&A pairs
dataset: 50,000+ domain-specific power electronics Q&A pairs (fine-tuning); 15,000+ technical documents indexed in FAISS (retrieval); data from NingboTech University course materials and textbooks
output_format: Conversational Q&A responses; personalized tutoring guidance; Socratic dialogue; resource recommendations; template-based generation with technical accuracy validation
evaluation_method: Quasi-experimental study with control group (n=180 students, 3 semesters, Autumn 2022-Autumn 2024); metrics: daily performance, final exam scores, lab report scores, project innovation ratings; statistical analysis with Cohen's d and p-values
educational_level: Higher education (undergraduate electrical engineering and automation)
key_findings: The three-part AI framework (RAG-based intelligent tutoring + AI-enhanced virtual simulation + AI-assisted project-based learning) significantly outperformed traditional instruction: ~19% increase in final exam scores, ~16% improvement in lab assessment scores, ~20% rise in project innovation ratings (all p<0.001, large effect sizes). Demonstrates that integrated RAG + fine-tuned LLM systems can effectively address theory-practice gaps in engineering education and deliver measurable learning gains.

title: PromptHive: Bringing Subject Matter Experts Back to the Forefront with Collaborative Prompt Engineering for Educational Content Creation
venue: Conf Hum Fact Comput Syst Proc
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2025 (CHI'25), in English, conference paper, uses LLMs (GPT-4o) to generate didactic materials (hints and scaffolds for algebra problems), and applies collaborative structured prompt engineering as adaptation technique CI5. Not a systematic review or medical study. Validated through a user study (n=10 experts, SUS=89/100) and a learning gain study (n=358 students). Relevant to RQ2 (quality of generated content), RQ3 (prompting techniques), RQ5 (evaluation), RQ6 (output formats: hints/scaffolds), RQ7 (human roles in the workflow), and RQ8 (HCI aspects of the authoring process).
adaptation_technique: Collaborative prompt engineering with subject matter expert (SME) involvement; structured 4-stage workflow (Load, Author, Iterate, Share) with a shared prompt library at textbook-level and lesson-level abstraction; self-consistency prompting; no fine-tuning or RAG
dataset: OpenStax College Algebra 2e textbook content via OATutor spreadsheet; 358 college learners for learning gain study; 10 SMEs for user study
output_format: Hints and scaffolds (plain hints and step-by-step scaffolded hints) in structured JSON format for integration into the OATutor adaptive tutoring system
evaluation_method: User study (n=10 SMEs): SUS usability scale (score=89/100), NASA-TLX cognitive load (55.17 to 26.73), XAI trust scale, semi-structured interviews; Learning gain RCT (n=358 students): comparison of PromptHive-generated hints vs. human-authored hints (8.13% vs 7.47% gain, p<0.001 vs. control; p=0.688 vs. human-only)
educational_level: College / university (college-level algebra, Open Adaptive Tutor context); also tested with middle school and high school math content
rq_coverage: RQ2; RQ3; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7; RQ8
key_findings: PromptHive allows subject matter experts (SMEs) to co-author prompts with LLMs to generate hints and scaffolds of quality comparable to human-written ones, reducing authoring time by a factor of 30 and subjective cognitive load by half. The learning gain study with 358 students showed that hints generated with PromptHive produce learning improvements statistically equivalent to manual hints (8.13% vs 7.47%, p=0.688), with excellent usability (SUS=89/100). The system shifts the expert's role from producer to curator: rather than writing content manually, experts guide, evaluate, and iterate on LLM outputs.

title: AI for All: Adaptive, Accessible, and Inclusive Learning Experiences in the Age of Intelligent LMSs
venue: Information (Switzerland)
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2026 (CI1), in English (CI2), journal article in MDPI Information (CI3), uses LLMs to generate multimodal didactic materials from a textbook (summaries, slide decks, quizzes, narrated media, chatbot) within an LMS (CI4), employs structured prompting and RAG as central techniques of the generative pipeline (CI5), and full text available (CI6). Not a systematic review or editorial. Not in the medical domain. The PREPARE system integrates a complete GenAI pipeline with adaptive personalisation and generation of multiple educational formats, closely aligned with the scope of this thesis.
llm_base: GPT-4 (implicit via OpenAI API); GenAI models not explicitly specified
adaptation_technique: Structured prompting (quota-aware orchestration, chapter-to-unit pipeline) + RAG (chatbot grounded in course material) + adaptive delivery based on learner profiling FSLSM/ILS + Moodle logs
dataset: University course PDF textbook (single authoritative source); student Moodle interactions for learner modeling
output_format: Chapter summaries, structured notes, slide decks, formative quiz items, video mini-lectures with captions, podcast-style audio, AR activities, conversational chatbot
evaluation_method: Mixed-methods: system-level validation (deployment completeness, instrumentation readiness, scalability), pre-registered evaluation design with metrics for engagement, interaction diversity, and perceived accessibility; empirical outcomes pending classroom deployment
educational_level: Higher education (university, deployed in Moodle with university students)
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_findings: PREPARE demonstrates that a GenAI end-to-end pipeline integrated into a mainstream LMS (Moodle) can transform a single PDF textbook into an adaptive multimodal learning ecosystem covering summaries, slides, quizzes, audio, AR, and a RAG chatbot, with non-prescriptive personalisation based on FSLSM and Moodle logs. System-level validation confirms technical feasibility and deployment readiness; reduction of instructor authoring effort and improvements in accessibility and inclusivity are expected outcomes pending empirical classroom validation. The system operates under GDPR compliance and anticipates the EU AI Act.

title: A Readability-Driven Prompting Framework for Accurate Grade-Specific EFL Narrative Creation
venue: International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2026 (CI1), in English (CI2), journal article in IJACSA (CI3), uses LLM (GPT-4o-mini) to generate narrative didactic materials in EFL (CI4), applies prompt engineering comparing three strategies (IBP, CKCP, and the proposed RDP) with an emphasis on readability-driven prompting (CI5), and full text available (CI6). Not a review or editorial. Proposes a novel prompting framework (Readability-Driven Prompting) that integrates readability constraints (Flesch-Kincaid, Dale-Chall), CEFR guidelines, and few-shot exemplars to control the difficulty level of narratives generated for EFL learners at 7th grade (A1-A2) and 10th grade (B1-B2).
adaptation_technique: Prompt engineering (few-shot readability-driven prompting with CEFR constraints and level exemplars; compared against Instruction-Based Prompting and CEFR Keyword-Constrained Prompting)
dataset: 600 synthetically generated narratives (3 methods x 2 grade levels x 100 narratives); human evaluation with 30 Japanese students from Tokyo Denki University
output_format: Educational narratives in English (text format, 200-300 words, EFL subject matter)
evaluation_method: Automatic readability metrics (Flesch-Kincaid Reading Ease, Dale-Chall); CEFR lexical analysis (SpaCy, NLTK, CEFR-J list); human evaluation survey (6 Likert items, n=30) in a mobile storytelling application (Unity)
educational_level: Secondary education (7th grade, A1-A2 CEFR; 10th grade, B1-B2 CEFR), EFL learners
key_findings: RDP outperforms IBP and is comparable to CKCP in readability control at the beginner level (7th grade): it is the only method that consistently falls within the target Flesch-Kincaid band of 70-79. At the intermediate level (10th grade), RDP is the only method that falls within the target FRE band of 50-59, although CKCP outperforms it on Dale-Chall. Human evaluation confirms that RDP narratives are comprehensible, natural, and motivating (means >= 4.2/5 on 5 of 6 items). The study shows that incorporating readability metrics and few-shot exemplars into the prompt enables scalable control of textual difficulty level without fine-tuning.

title: AdaptLearn AI: A Generative AI Framework for Bidirectional Personalization in Education
venue: Int. Conf. Compu., Intell., Appl., CIACON
justification: The article presents AdaptLearn AI, a generative AI framework for K-12 and higher education that uses GPT-4 (with RAG and few-shot prompting) and LLaMA-2-7B (with 4-bit quantisation) to generate personalised didactic materials (rubrics, STEM exercises, lesson summaries, IEPs). Meets all inclusion criteria: LLMs as a central component (CI1), clear educational context (CI2), documented adaptation methods (RAG + few-shot prompting + lightweight fine-tuning) (CI3), explicit generation of didactic materials (CI4), published in 2025 (CI5), and accessible via IEEE Xplore with USAL licence (CI6). No exclusion criteria apply.
adaptation_technique: RAG; few-shot prompting; fine-tuning (4-bit quantization); chain-of-thought prompting; zero-shot generalization
fine_tuning_method: LLaMA-2-7B with 4-bit quantization via Hugging Face TGI (lightweight fine-tuning for teacher tools); GPT-4 used via API with structured prompting
dataset: Synthetic cohort of 50 GPT-4 personas; pilot with 5 teachers and 15 students in 5 high schools; 100 essays evaluated by humans as gold standard; syllabus PDFs from real curriculum
output_format: assessment rubrics; adaptive STEM exercises; lesson summaries; individualised education plans (IEP); automated feedback
evaluation_method: BERTScore (F1); nDCG@5 for RAG pipeline; Likert-scale surveys (teacher/student satisfaction); API latency benchmarks; expert review (Bloom's taxonomy alignment); A/B pilot testing
educational_level: K-12 and higher education (primary focus on STEM, high school, and university)
rq_coverage: RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_findings: AdaptLearn AI achieves 89% satisfaction in pilots, a 37% reduction in teacher planning time, and a 22% improvement in quiz scores compared to workflows without AI. The RAG pipeline reaches nDCG@5=0.89, a 23.6% improvement over baseline. The bidirectional architecture, based on real-time synchronisation between the teacher module (RAG+GPT-4) and the student module (few-shot LLaMA), demonstrates synergistic benefits: reducing the teacher's administrative load amplifies student engagement by 22%. The 4-bit quantisation of LLaMA-2-7B enables local deployment with inference control, reducing latency to 210ms (p95).

title: Building Intelligent Educational Agents: A Scalable LLM-Based Framework for Assessment Generation
venue: Procedia Comput. Sci.
justification: The paper proposes ClassQuiz, a scalable LLM-based framework that uses fine-tuning, prompt engineering, and RAG to automatically generate educational assessments (quizzes/exercises) in an educational context. It implements a concrete method with GPT variants and BERT, evaluates output quality, and targets personalized assessment generation for students.
adaptation_technique: fine-tuning; RAG; prompting
fine_tuning_method: SFT
dataset: Conversations dataset built from educational materials and past assessments (domain-specific corpus for ClassQuiz platform)
output_format: quiz; exercise
evaluation_method: human; LLM-judge
educational_level: general
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7; RQ8
key_findings: The ClassQuiz framework combining fine-tuned GPT, prompt engineering, and RAG can generate high-quality, contextually relevant educational assessments tailored to individual student profiles and learning goals. Future work highlights limitations including lack of multimodal support and the need for more sophisticated fine-tuning to improve contextual relevance.

title: Agentic AI Quiz-Based Learning System: Enhancing MCQ Generation via Long-Context Cached Retrieval-Augmented Generation
venue: Proc. Front. Educ. Conf. FIE
justification: Conference paper (FIE 2025) that directly addresses LLM-based MCQ generation for engineering education using an agentic RAG architecture with long-context caching. Satisfies CI1 (2025), CI2 (English), CI3 (conference paper), CI4 (LLM for didactic material generation), CI5 (RAG + prompting: Corpus-in-Context and context caching), and CI6 (full text available). No medical/health content. Not a review/survey.
llm_base: Gemini 1.5 Flash (1M-token context); GPT-4o; Claude Sonnet 3.7; Grok-3 (dataset generation)
adaptation_technique: Agentic RAG with long-context caching; Corpus-in-Context (CiC) prompting; prompt caching (100k-1M token cache); metadata-conditioned prompt engineering by difficulty level
dataset: 4,404 MCQs on multidisciplinary AI topics (Machine Learning, Generative AI, Neural Networks); curated from SanFoundry, Simplilearn, InterviewBit, HackerKareer, Kaggle; expanded with Grok-3 deterministic generation; split 80/20 train/test (3,523 / 881 questions)
output_format: Multiple-choice questions (MCQs) with question stem, four choices, correct answer, explanation, difficulty level, and topic metadata
evaluation_method: Exact Match, Faithfulness, Hallucination rate, Explanation Similarity (BERTScore, BLEU, ROUGE), Context Recall, Uncertainty/Certainty; McNemar's test for statistical significance; Cohen's d for effect size; 150-question benchmark subset
educational_level: Higher education / engineering undergraduates (FAANG-level AI interview preparation)
key_findings: Long-context cached RAG (1M-token Gemini Flash) achieved 93.33% MCQ accuracy vs. 78% baseline (raw), with progressive gains through chunking (84%) and caching (89.33%). High Faithfulness (0.896) and ExactMatch (0.933) confirm reliable answer generation. GPT-4o reached 94% (highest accuracy) and Claude 3.7 led in Certainty (0.9). Combining long-context windows with prompt caching and modular agentic orchestration mitigates RAG fragmentation and hallucination, enabling scalable, personalized quiz generation for engineering education.

title: LLM-based Maritime Training Feedback System: Implementing RAG-Enhanced Assessment Analysis with STCW Compliance
venue: TransNav
justification: Journal article (2025, TransNav) in English presenting a RAG-enhanced automated feedback system for maritime professional training. Uses Mistral-7B optimized with QLoRA plus RAG (FAISS vector store) and prompt engineering (zero-shot, few-shot, structured templates) to generate STCW-compliant feedback on assessment questions. Covers CI4 (LLM for didactic material generation/feedback), CI5 (RAG + QLoRA + prompting), and all formal inclusion criteria. Domain is maritime vocational education, not medical/health, so CE3 does not apply.
adaptation_technique: RAG (FAISS vector store with STCW competency documents) + QLoRA (4-bit quantization, LoRA rank=8 alpha=16 targeting attention modules) + prompt engineering (zero-shot, few-shot, structured templates)
fine_tuning_method: QLoRA (parameter-efficient fine-tuning via 4-bit quantization with Low-Rank Adaptation for local deployment optimization)
dataset: STCW competency documents and maritime assessment questions structured into a FAISS vector store; student assessment responses used for evaluation
output_format: Structured automated feedback (text) for multiple-choice and short essay maritime assessment questions; includes answer diagnosis graphs for essay responses
evaluation_method: STCW concept coverage metric (% of key STCW concepts present in generated feedback; structured templates achieved 85%); response time benchmarks (<15s for all questions); expert maritime instructor ratings for feedback quality; comparative analysis across zero-shot, few-shot, and structured prompt templates
educational_level: Higher/professional vocational education (maritime training for seafarers under STCW Convention standards)
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_findings: RAG-enhanced Mistral-7B with QLoRA generates STCW-compliant feedback achieving 85% concept coverage with response times under 15 seconds. QLoRA reduced memory requirements from >14GB to ~4GB enabling consumer-grade hardware deployment. Structured prompt templates outperform zero-shot (65%) and few-shot (78%) approaches for regulatory compliance. RAG retrieval of domain-specific regulatory content is essential for standards-aligned feedback in specialized educational domains. Prompt engineering design is critical: structured templates produce pedagogically sound, organized feedback superior to simpler prompting strategies.

title: EduCraft: A System for Generating Pedagogical Lecture Scripts from Long-Context Multimodal Presentations
venue: CIKM - Proc. ACM Int. Conf. Info. Knowl. Manage.
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2025 (CIKM), in English, ACM conference article, uses LLMs (GPT-4V, Qwen2.5-VL-72B, GPT-4o, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, DeepSeek-V3) to generate didactic material (lecture scripts from multimodal presentations), applies structured prompting (instruction-guided workflows) and optional RAG, with automatic and human evaluation of educational quality. Not a review, not an editorial, not medical.
llm_base: GPT-4V; Qwen2.5-VL-72B; GPT-4o; Claude 3.7 Sonnet; DeepSeek-V3
adaptation_technique: Instruction-guided prompting with VLM workflow (holistic VLM) and Caption+LLM workflow (two-stage); optional RAG for external factual grounding
dataset: 20 university presentations from the MAIC platform (10 English, 10 Chinese), 272 slides in total; human evaluation with 20 presentations from 4 university courses (320 slides)
output_format: Lecture scripts (structured classroom scripts for oral presentation), narrative text format per slide with global coherence
evaluation_method: Comparative human evaluation (ranking by 10 annotators on Consistency, Readability, Coherence); multi-dimensional automatic metrics: Content Relevance, Expressive Clarity, Logical Structure, Audience Engagement, Narrative Flow, Information Hierarchy, Conceptual Integration, Thematic Consistency, Cross-referencing
educational_level: Higher education (university courses across various disciplines: Humanities, STEM, Social Sciences)
key_findings: EduCraft significantly outperforms iterative baselines and teacher-refined scripts on all three human evaluation metrics (Consistency, Readability, Coherence). The modular architecture with holistic pedagogical instruction, rather than slide-by-slide processing, is key for maintaining long-range coherence. Optional RAG integration improves factuality but may reduce narrative clarity. The Caption+LLM workflow yields competitive results with greater model flexibility. The ablation study confirms that the Multimodal Content Grounding module is the most critical component (impact -0.11 on Comb).

title: EduAgent: A Multimodal Chatbot Framework for Enhancing Interactive Learning in Electrical and Electronics Engineering Education
venue: International Journal of Modern Education and Computer Science
justification: Journal article (2026, English) presenting EduAgent, a multimodal chatbot for Electrical and Electronic Engineering education. Applies supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on Llama-3.1-8B and Llama-3.2-3B with a proprietary conversational dataset of 596 four-turn examples, along with few-shot prompting and RAG-style image retrieval (BM25 + TF-IDF). Meets CI1-CI6: year 2026, English, journal article, LLM for education, FT+prompting+retrieval, full text available. Not an editorial, not a systematic review, not medical.
adaptation_technique: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) with a synthetic conversational dataset generated by GPT-4o; few-shot prompting (zero-shot, one-shot, three-shot); image retrieval via BM25 and LangChain TF-IDF Retriever (RAG-like)
fine_tuning_method: SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning) with implicit PEFT/LoRA (adamw 8bit optimizer, efficient fine-tuning parameters; PEFT/LoRA mentioned in the background section)
dataset: Proprietary dataset: 596 four-turn conversations on EEE topics (Electronic Components, Power Electronics, Amplifiers, Digital Electronics, Transistors, etc.) generated with GPT-4o; 118 EEE images collected from GeekForGeeks, Hackatronic, Researchgate
output_format: Multimodal responses: step-by-step pedagogical text plus a relevant image retrieved from a vector store
evaluation_method: Automatic metrics: BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore; human evaluation by educators and subject-matter experts on 4 dimensions: correctness, completeness, conciseness, engagement; image evaluation on relevance and understandability
educational_level: Undergraduate (Electrical and Electronic Engineering, university level)
rq_coverage: RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_findings: EduAgent combines SFT on Llama-3 with few-shot prompting and an image retriever to generate multimodal pedagogical responses in EEE. Few-shot prompting with Llama-3.1-8B outperformed GPT-4o on conciseness and engagement according to human evaluation. The TF-IDF retriever from LangChain was superior to BM25 for relevant image retrieval. The system still lacks individual personalisation, proposed as future work together with improved mathematical support.

title: Personalized Assessment in Education: Enhancing MCQ Generation Using Advanced AI and Contextual Learning Models
venue: Int. Conf. Circuits, Controls Commun., CCUBE
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: (CI1) published in 2025 at CCUBE 2025; (CI2) in English; (CI3) IEEE conference article; (CI4) proposes the PAMGen framework for automatic generation of personalised educational MCQs using LLMs; (CI5) employs RAG for external knowledge retrieval, advanced prompting (Chain-of-Thought and Self-Refine), and GPT-4 as the base model; (CI6) full text available. Not a review or survey, not medical. The topic fits directly within the scope of the TFM: generation of didactic material (MCQs) with LLMs + RAG + structured prompting + personalisation according to student profile.
adaptation_technique: RAG + prompt engineering (Chain-of-Thought reasoning, Self-Refine prompting)
dataset: Curated dataset of MCQs designed by instructors and generated by students, categorised by difficulty (Bloom's taxonomy) and domain; dynamic external knowledge base retrieved via RAG
output_format: Multiple-choice questions (MCQs) with stem, correct answer, and distractors; categories: math-based, concept-based, coding-based
evaluation_method: Human evaluation (3 subject-matter experts) and automatic evaluation (LLM-as-judge) on 5 criteria: Grammatical Fluency, Answerability, Diversity, Complexity, Relevance
educational_level: Higher education / university (engineering, computer science)
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6
key_findings: PAMGen combines GPT-4 with RAG and prompting techniques (CoT and Self-Refine) to generate personalised MCQs adapted to difficulty and pedagogical objectives. Concept-based and coding-based MCQs achieve high scores in grammatical fluency and relevance in both human and automatic evaluation. Math-based MCQs show lower answerability and complexity, indicating an area for improvement. The adaptive feedback mechanism allows questions to be iteratively refined according to student performance, producing a personalised and scalable learning experience.

title: Research on the construction and application of intelligent tutoring system for english teaching based on generative pre-training model
venue: Systems and Soft Computing
justification: Journal article (2025, English) that constructs an intelligent tutoring system for English teaching using GPT-based generative pre-training models with fine-tuning on English teaching domain data. Directly addresses LLM adaptation (CI5: fine-tuning), didactic material generation (CI4), personalization via learner profiles, and multi-format output (exercises, dialogues, feedback, summaries). All inclusion criteria met.
llm_base: GPT-based generative pre-training model; BERT; RoBERTa; BART; UniLM; T5; Pegasus
adaptation_technique: Fine-tuning of GPT-based pre-trained models on English teaching domain data; learner-profile-driven adaptive difficulty adjustment
fine_tuning_method: Supervised fine-tuning of generative pre-trained models (BERT/UniLM/GPT-based) on downstream English teaching tasks (text classification, similarity, generation); UNILMv2 fine-tuning with LSTM fusion
dataset: Public English corpora (BNC, COCA); student learning data from online platforms and offline test scores; CNN/DailyMail and XSum datasets for evaluation
output_format: Exercises (grammar, vocabulary, listening, speaking, writing), dialogue-based tutoring, instant feedback and corrections, reading materials and abstracts, personalized learning paths
evaluation_method: ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L metrics on CNN/DailyMail and XSum; ablation study on system modules; empirical user study measuring improvement in reading comprehension (+35%), writing (+30%), speaking fluency (+25%), learner engagement (+40%), learning satisfaction (+40%)
educational_level: Higher education / adult English language learners (university context implied; system generalizable)
rq_coverage: RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_findings: A GPT-based ITS for English teaching fine-tuned on domain data builds detailed learner profiles (proficiency scores, learning history, self-reported preferences) and dynamically generates personalized exercises and feedback. Fine-tuned generative pre-training models outperform BART, Pegasus, T5, and FASUM baselines on ROUGE metrics. The system achieves +35% reading comprehension, +30% writing skill, +25% speaking fluency, and +40% learner engagement improvement over traditional teaching. Semantic perceptual attention and knowledge graph modules are key contributors to output quality.

title: Intelligent teaching design assistant for primary mathematics: A large language model-driven framework with retrieval-augmented generation and problem-chain pedagogy
venue: International Electronic Journal of Mathematics Education
justification: Journal article (2026, CI1+CI3) in English (CI2) with full text available (CI6). Proposes an instructional design assistant system for primary school mathematics based on LLMs with RAG and a multi-agent architecture (CI4+CI5). Uses RAG over curricular knowledge bases, pedagogical strategies, and student profiles to generate lesson plans of superior quality compared to generic LLMs (CI5). Not a review and not a medical article.
llm_base: GPT (version unspecified, accessed via the Coze platform); generic Large Language Model as baseline
adaptation_technique: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) with multiple knowledge bases (curriculum, teaching strategies, student profiles); multi-agent architecture (process planner + student simulator); problem-chain pedagogy prompting
dataset: Ad hoc knowledge bases: Curriculum Knowledge Base (curricular standards), Teaching Strategy Base (pedagogical strategies), Student Learning Style Base (student profiles); content from Grade 5 primary school mathematics (unit: multiplication of decimals)
output_format: Lesson plan / teaching design (exportable Word document) structured with teaching objectives, problem chains, activities, and assessment
evaluation_method: Quasi-experiment with 34 primary school teachers (N=34): comparison of Task 1 (generic LLM) vs Task 2 (intelligent assistant); evaluation by 3 expert teachers using a multidimensional rubric (objectives, content, strategies, problem chains, activities, usability, assessment); post-use TAM questionnaire (5-point Likert, 14 items); semi-structured interviews; paired t-test
educational_level: Primary education (5th grade, 11-12 years old)
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ6; RQ7
key_findings: The intelligent assistant significantly outperformed the generic LLM on overall lesson plan quality (Task 2: M=5.83 vs Task 1: M=3.67, p<0.01). The greatest improvements were observed in problem-chain design (ΔM=2.45), teaching objectives (ΔM=2.07), and usability (ΔM=2.99). Teachers reported high ease of use (M=4.72) and satisfaction (M=4.29). RAG over curricular knowledge bases was the key factor in reducing cognitive load and increasing the contextual accuracy of the generated content. Limitations: small sample, single grade and subject.

title: RAG Technology to Develop and Evaluate an Automatic Generation System for Similar Problems with Adjustable Difficulty
venue: Proc. - Int. Conf. Inf. Technol., InCIT
justification: Conference paper (2025, IEEE InCIT) that directly addresses LLM+RAG for automatic generation of analogous educational problems with adjustable difficulty. Uses GPT-4.1 via RAG with few-shot prompting and user-subjective difficulty feedback. Meets CI1 (2025), CI2 (EN), CI3 (conference paper), CI4 (LLM for didactic material generation), CI5 (RAG + prompting), CI6 (full text available). No medical/health content. Covers RQ2 (RAG), RQ3 (prompt engineering/few-shot), RQ5 (output format: MCQ), RQ6 (quality/validity evaluation), RQ7 (difficulty-based personalization).
adaptation_technique: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) with cosine similarity threshold for chunk retrieval + few-shot prompting using user-approved conforming problems as examples
dataset: Custom Background Knowledge DB built from first-year university information science course materials (educational materials chunked at item/section level, vectorized with OpenAI text-embedding-3-large); Conforming Problems and Subjective Evaluations DB of user-validated analogous problems
output_format: Multiple-choice questions (MCQ) with two correct answers, answer options, and explanation
evaluation_method: Conforming problem rate (difficulty agreement on 7-point subjective scale); problem validity rate (correctness of answer count, clarity, format adherence); quality assessment on 5-point scale (similarity, scope adherence, naturalness); experiments: preliminary threshold optimization + author self-validation + third-party evaluation with 3 students
educational_level: University undergraduate (first-year information science courses)
key_findings: RAG with cosine similarity threshold 0.5 achieves 100% scope/naturalness adherence and 90.5% problem validity rate. Incorporating user subjective difficulty evaluations as few-shot examples improves difficulty conforming rate from 38.1% (diagnostic phase) to 81.0% (exercise phase). The feedback loop progressively calibrates difficulty to the user's subjective intent, reducing instructor burden in problem creation while maintaining educational scope integrity.

title: Optimization and Application of Generative AI Algorithm Based on Transformer Architecture in Adaptive Learning
venue: Information (Switzerland)
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2026 (CI1), in English (CI2), journal article in Information MDPI (CI3), applies LLM (LLaMA-2-13B) to generate personalised didactic content in an adaptive learning system (CI4), uses fine-tuning with LoRA + instruction fine-tuning + dynamic prompting (CI5), full text available (CI6). Not a review and not an editorial.
adaptation_technique: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) + instruction fine-tuning + dynamic prompt construction
fine_tuning_method: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation, rank=8) applied to the Q and V attention matrices; supervised instruction fine-tuning with a proprietary educational dataset; no RLHF
dataset: Proprietary educational dataset built ad hoc: approximately 150,000 text fragments (mathematics, physics, English; Chinese secondary education) obtained from digital educational platforms, question banks, and student logs
output_format: Generated text: adaptive responses to questions, explanations, and personalised feedback on student errors
evaluation_method: BLEU, ROUGE-L (>0.9); quasi-experimental pre/post-test with 34 students in the experimental group vs. 34 in the control group; response latency and resource utilisation metrics
educational_level: Secondary education (junior high to senior high school)
rq_coverage: RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_findings: The method integrates LoRA fine-tuning, instruction fine-tuning, and a dynamic prompt mechanism on LLaMA-2-13B to generate personalised educational content. It surpasses BLEU and ROUGE-L > 0.9 and reduces latency compared to traditional fine-tuning. The experimental group achieved a post-test score of 82.4 versus 76.1 for the control group. The system incorporates a wrong question guidance module and knowledge graph embedding for intelligent feedback. Limitations include deficiencies in multimodal fusion and modelling of long-term learning trajectories.

title: Aduvia: A Multi-Purpose AI-Powered Learning Management System (LMS)
venue: Novel Intell. Lead. Emerg. Sci. Conf., NILES - Proc.
justification: The article presents Aduvia, an AI-powered LMS that uses Llama 3.3 as its central LLM combined with RAG, structured prompt engineering, and fine-tuning for educational interactions. It generates didactic material (automatic quizzes, personalised contextual responses) in a real educational environment. Meets CI1 (Llama 3.3 as central LLM), CI2 (explicit educational context), CI3 (RAG + prompt engineering + fine-tuning), CI4 (quiz generation and didactic dialogue), CI5 (NILES2025), and CI6 (access via IEEE Xplore/USAL). Not a review (CE2), not biomedical (CE3), and the LLM is indispensable to the main contribution (not CE5).
adaptation_technique: RAG; prompt engineering; fine-tuning; semantic chunking; e5-base-v2 embeddings
fine_tuning_method: Fine-tuning not specified in detail; mentioned as 'fine-tuned for educational interactions' alongside RAG as the main adaptation technique
dataset: Educational content uploaded by users (PDFs, images, slides); processed via OCR (Tesseract v4.1.1) and semantic chunking; stored in FAISS with metadata in MongoDB
output_format: Conversational dialogue; automatic quizzes; contextual responses in HTML/MathJax
evaluation_method: Cosine similarity (e5-base-v2 vs MiniLM); precision/recall/F1 on relevance classification (P=0.85, R=0.92, F1=0.88); response latency benchmarks (streaming vs non-streaming); comparison of Llama 3.3 vs Llama 3.0
educational_level: Not specified (higher education implicitly assumed from the university context of the authors and the use of an LMS)
key_findings: Aduvia demonstrates that RAG with Llama 3.3 and e5-base-v2 embeddings outperforms MiniLM in semantic retrieval (F1=0.88 vs threshold 0.65) and reduces latency by 30-40% compared to Llama 3.0. The integration of semantic chunking, FAISS indexing, and structured prompt engineering enables personalised educational dialogue generation and real-time automatic quizzes within a unified LMS pipeline.

title: Dynamic-rag: a multimodal emotion-aware prompting and retrieval-augmented LLM framework for autism support in adaptive learning systems
venue: Quality and Quantity
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: published in 2026 (CI1), in English (CI2), journal article in Quality & Quantity (CI3), uses LLMs to generate and adapt personalised educational material for autistic learners (CI4), combines RAG and dynamic prompting as main techniques (CI5), full text available (CI6). No exclusion criterion applies: it is not an editorial or review/survey (it is a system study with empirical evaluation), the scope is not clinical medical/health (it is inclusive education/neurodevelopment), and LLMs are used as a central component.
adaptation_technique: Adaptive RAG (state-conditioned retrieval with dynamic depth k∈{3,5,7}) + Dynamic Prompting Engine (real-time adjustment of instructions, difficulty and tone based on multimodal Pedagogical Readiness Score)
dataset: GSM8K (elementary school mathematics, grades 3-5); domain-specific knowledge corpus retrieved via BM25+TF-IDF; real-time multimodal signals (EEG theta/beta, eye-tracking, facial valence-arousal) used to compute the PRS
output_format: Explanatory textual responses adapted in length, structure and conceptual complexity (scaffolded vs. abstract) according to the learner's readiness level
evaluation_method: Exact Match (EM), Token F1, TF-IDF Semantic Similarity; hallucination rate (binary manual annotation by two experts, Cohen's κ=0.84); pedagogical alignment score; bootstrap CI (B=2000); McNemar's test; retrieval latency
educational_level: Primary (grades 3-5, mathematics); target population: students with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)
key_findings: DYNAMIC-RAG combines RAG with a dynamic prompting engine guided by a multimodal Pedagogical Readiness Score (PRS) covering engagement, valence-arousal, and clinical indicators. Retrieval depth and generation parameters adapt in real time to the learner's cognitive-affective state. Compared to a baseline GPT-4o-mini: +17.5 pp in Exact Match (47.5% to 65%), +0.068 Token F1, and a 68.2% reduction in hallucinations (45% to 14.3%). Low-readiness learners benefit more (+28.6 pp) than high-readiness ones (+9.1 pp). Mean latency is 1.48 s, preserving real-time response. The study is a proof-of-concept (n=40 queries); the authors acknowledge the need for confirmatory validation at larger scale.

title: Empowering Education with AI: Automating Content Generation through Large Language Models
venue: International Journal of Information and Education Technology
justification: Meets all inclusion criteria: (CI1) GPT-4 is the central LLM of the system; (CI2) explicit educational context covering ITS, lesson plans, quizzes, and study guides; (CI3) empirical method with quantitative evaluation (96% accuracy) and an educator survey (85% adoption); (CI4) direct generation of multi-format educational material adapted to the learner; (CI5) published in 2025; (CI6) Open Access CC BY 4.0. The techniques used (fine-tuning and prompt engineering on GPT-4, with RAG and RLHF mentioned as future work) and the evaluation of personalisation and quality directly address the mapping's PICOC and RQs.
adaptation_technique: fine-tuning; prompt engineering; RAG (mentioned as future work); RLHF (mentioned as future work)
fine_tuning_method: Fine-tuning of GPT-4 for specific educational tasks using domain data; prompt iteration for output refinement
dataset: Not explicitly specified; curated educational domain data; human annotators used to mitigate biases in training data
output_format: lesson plans; quizzes; study guides; complete curricula; interactive exercises; personalised feedback
evaluation_method: accuracy rate (96% validated by educators); teacher adoption survey (85% likelihood); human evaluation by educators; alignment with Bloom's taxonomy
educational_level: Multiple levels: primary, secondary and higher education; cognitive complexity adapted to the student's level
rq_coverage: RQ1; RQ2; RQ3; RQ4; RQ5; RQ6; RQ7
key_findings: A GPT-4-based system with fine-tuning and prompt engineering achieved 96% accuracy in educational content generation as validated by teachers, and an 85% probability of adoption. The system generates lesson plans, quizzes, and study guides adapted to the student's cognitive level following Bloom's taxonomy, demonstrating scalability and personalisation. LMS integration via API is feasible. The main challenges are occasional hallucinations, biases in training data, and unequal technological access. Future work includes RAG, RLHF, and multimodal inputs.
